author: Markellwhite16
content: TBH you should really focus on HTF key levels , mark out your key levels from the weekly , daily , 4hr , and PDH/PDL . once you do this you can take trades off these HTF key levels because they hold more liquidity , volume , these are levels where big money is sitting etc. AND it will help you avoid taking trades . you went LONG into a major R level on NVDA. Here is  SS , look at all this overhead resistance. i see why you took the trade, i was actually looking at this too in real time BUT what made me pass was these HTF key levels. At the end of the day do what works best for YOU! i do not trade or mark out pre market levels. those levels are created during the after hours and pre market , that being said those levels dont have much volume behind them so they dont hold as much weight and conviction as these HTF (higher time frame) key levels. really hope this helps!

author: 305 Trader
content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

author: jav
content: Hey Max, I am certainly not someone to be giving advice but somethings I have learned and advice I have been given that you may find useful. In regards to a mental stop or a hard stop, with a hard stop if the candle hits your stop area your going to be stopped out of the trade, which is why Tony and Jack always say to wait for candle closure. I used to always use a hard stop but I would mostly get stopped out and then the stock would take off in the way I wanted it to, which can be very nerve racking. I am not sure what your set up was on those 2 trades but Tony advises you to stay with one set up when you are a new trader, whether that be the 5 minute opening range, premarket or previous day and just stay with whichever your most comfortable with. I personally only trade the 5 minute opening range break. I also switched to a 5 minute chart because trading off a 1 minute chart I found to be super stressful. The only con to a 5 minute chart when waiting for candle closure is if you get a big candle the opposite of your trade your stop is going to be much lower than you wanted it to be, but that has only happened once in TSLA.  I hope you found something useful in this and good luck in your trading journey.

author: FierceTiger
content: Price moves because it seeks liquidity. When PM high gets taken out, there is no more liquidity there and price moves in the direction where the next draw on liquidity is, which is a lower level. Hence you go short. I hope this helps.

author: FierceTiger
content: No, it is too simplistic to say that. But generally, the PMH and PML, when taken out by the first few candles, determines the direction of the initial big move at market open (until the next level of liquidity is taken out.)

author: 305 Trader
content: Setting up the correct stop loss (where the trade invalidates) at start is a real art guys and sticking to it with a mechanical stop loss is the only way to create a good habit of accepting the risk of trading. No hope, just a mechanical trade plan implemented with discipline. Mental stop losses do not help us create good habits. They are helpful when the disciplined trader has become the operator. Before this, mental stop losses are a demon for me.

author: FierceTiger
content: I entered as soon as I confirmed CISD on the 2 minute TF. My point of invalidation was high of day. Note that this is an A+ setup. Accumulation and manipulation was very clear from the pre-market - I only caught the distribution leg and rode it on the way down. Once this setup is formed, the chances of it reversing and stopping me out is extremely low. Also, note that you usually don't get a PO3 every time in the premarket with the premarket high getting taken out on the first few candles at market open. Sometimes PM high does not get taken out and it goes down anyway (this would not be A+, as it can reverse anytime). However once the PO3 short setup with PM high taken out presents itself, it is a bread-and-butter A+ setup.

Very bad day today regarding PnL (-1988$), good day regarding "respect the process". I saw the trades through until they hit SL. Than I added a fourth trade only because I am still on paper and I want to use this state to try out what comes up in my brain. As I am a very bad rule holder I decided last week to implement only one rule after the other: First I did risk managment (don't stay in the trade after candle close beneath SL), next is: only exit when SL or PT is hit. In other words: don't look at the PnL, look at the process. Well, today that wasn't a nice thing to do but I don't feel really bad with such a loss on paper anymore. That means I don't identify my personal value with the outcome of a trade anymore. 
The fourth trade I took was out of the "take only three trades a day" rule but I allowed it due to that I am still on paper and I did not implement that rule yet. That rule will be the last one that I will implement while being on paper trading. The most important rule for me now is: Stay in the trade until it hits PT. My wining stats are good, I just exited too early up to now because I rather took a few dollars win then loosing a trade. This is going to change!

author: 305 Trader

A month ago, I would have been the "hopeful trader" and possibly I could have wiped out my small account. But my trade plan got activated in regards to the stop loss and it was executed. The 2nd trade was applying a rule of the market that says: "after a failed signal, a stronger one to the opposite direction follows". Only problem was that today is an atypical day. So, because atypical reactions can happen, a stop loss that is clear and accepted must be in place.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Sean, I think this question somewhat depends on how you saw the setup / entry. I'm guessing you were trying the retest off the HOD / 5m ORH? If it were me, I'd have drawn out the full 1m OB since we had more displacement from there and structurally is the area price could dig back into. From my perspective the ideal entry on this trade is the reclaim of that OB /HOD on the 9:45 candle with a stop below the full OB. 

There wasn't really any 'sequence' on the pullback on your entry, more of an immediate retest - I don't usually take those trades but if I did I'd probably manage it more aggressively with a break below the retest level; one of those - 'has to work right away' situations IMO. 

But I think you have it right - structurally the invalidation looks to me is below the low of that 9:33 downclose candle

author: FierceTiger
content: Can I ask what is your edge and what kind of setups do you trade? As for me, I learned that I do better and have a higher success rate in shorting rather than longing. The reason is I have trouble maintaining my position when going long and reading potential reversals (which can happen anytime). I tend to panic sell as soon as I see that first red candle, and can't hold longs for long. For shorts, it's usually an impulsive "elevator down" kind of move which makes reversals easier to read (i.e. when the move starts to lose momentum and has a change of market structure). So until that point, it's easier for me to hold onto my shorts to capture a bigger move. I sometimes trade obvious intraday reversals, but my current bread-and-butter and edge is shorting certain setups at market open and catching the initial move down.

So I would take my entry after a pullback which took place 4 candles later and adjust my stop loss accordingly. also interesting to see what the 5 min TF was saying.

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1047881373370818610> , 0DTE forces you to have a quality of an entry. You still can have a narrow stop loss as you did. But in my opinion, as someone who only trades 0DTE, you need a clear confirmation candle and a strong set up. You did not have either one at your entry: No displacement at near ATH, and bars with long wicks which tell you market is indecisive. Still it would have worked out but with a larger stop loss such as below the Low of the green candle that brokeout your KL. This is part of the problem of weak set ups, that they need a large stop loss for them to work.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi <@1398013674194468996> great question I often asked myself. It depends on how much attention and focus you need to take a trade. Think of it as a High-jump jumper in a track & field competition. Even though they are top performers, the tournament requires that they take a break in between their three attempts to make their best high jump. If you alread won in 1x trade, I would take a break and reflect on what happened, drink water. Only after my break I may come back to the charts. But now, think of actually the downside. and this one is critical. What do you do if you lose on the 1st trade? This is an even more interesting challenge, most people including some coaches here will be tempted to revenge trade and they will actually take more trades, with that loss in mind. This is a bigger mistake or offense to your own trading plan and system.

author: Lou_13
content: Okay... so if I take one trade i need to reflect because I can lose what i already made and could be avoided

content:
Yes correct. Thats what i always do. Context n reference towards indices are very important.

author: terenceght
content:
I look at key levels like pivot levels, previous day levels, HTF levels. 

So for example, i want to go Long on a certain stock. I will look at where QQQ is at. If its bouncing on key support levels then it will give me additional confirmation to go Long. 
But i will not go Short when QQQ is at key support levels (again referring back to the TSLA trade that you mentioned initially)

author: Kam
content:
https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1219022389396901958/1427819217780211732 <@648855504994566154> moving the AMD trade discussion to this channel. Attaching some markup also in case it helps. There were a couple of things that kept me out earlier (I'm assuming you entered at 10:42?). 

1) QQQ context. Q's were struggling at PDL and I wanted to see a reclaim of PDL and ideally PMH for any longs at that point. AMD also wasn't moving as strongly as the indices (QQQ was well above HOD and AMD was barely trying to break above)

2) After 10:15 EST I stop looking at the 1m chart for entries and have a rule to only take 5m candle closures - especially on reclaims. 

3) Something Tony has drilled into my head - "where does the momo start"? For AMD my PM plan was to look for long setups over PMH with the gap-up. We weren't at PMH so started looking at intraday stucture for momo evidence. Since we hadn't displaced above HOD level (other than PM), 10:42 was more of a breakout trade to me (vs a reclaim). When the 5m didn't close above HOD in the morning session - the wicks from those attempts at 222 became the momentum level to me where buyers likely trapped 

4) Set an alert at 222 and only entered when 5m closed above that HOD pivot - note the QQQ context was much better at noon EST also (Above all intraday pivots and trending). Because AMD had attempted to reclaim at 11:40 but gave those trap wicks, once we closed above 222 had even more conviction that could push to PMH / PDH

5) Side-note - for a reclaim trade I'd still say this was a B-grade setup because of the lack of an impulse candle on the 5m, and relative weakness to QQQ - sort of trusted the HTFs and all the other things mentioned 

Sorry for all the detail - helps me walk back through it also, so I appreciate the question!

author: 305 Trader
content: I like displacement and a clear TP to aim for safely as often as I can. That Higher high was excellent target (in purple), the move had demonstrated going up at my black circle.

author: 305 Trader
content: In a healthy uptrend, the job of a lower high is to create a higher high. At the top of higher highs there is always resistance. So you like to enter near a new lower high and aim for the previous higher high, that is the ascending staircase of an uptrend.

author: 305 Trader
content: higher lows’ job is to create new higher highs. We want to enter near higher lows and aim for the higher highs, take profit there and continue riding.

author: 305 Trader
content: If no more riding, a ChOCH occured, then we exit fast with the bounty of that TP taken like a thief out of a bank cause your driver was waiting with the engine running.

author: Kam
content:
No worries, glad it helped. Love that attitude, keep it up! 

Definitely backtest the 5m closures so that you can find what works for your style/system. 

Waiting for 5m closes can widen the stops sometimes and kill the R/R so stop placement is pretty critical and keep time of day top of mind. 

For stops, focus on the level that's being reclaimed (if trading a reclaim). I used to be really bad  at reclaims when I first joined this room (ask <@1062934985109160057> 😂), but now they're becoming one of my best setups so definitely keep practicing! 

Generally from my data it's ok for a level to be retested after a reclaim, but these setups should move pretty quickly so don't want to give too much room.

author: 305 Trader
content:
I wanted to share how I am trading the indices in this ATH levels with no references.

- If trading before 10:45 EST which I do not do, use Fibonacci extensions. I am observing they are respected. 

- Trading only after 11 am to allow for intraday key levels in 10, 15 and 30 mins. 

- No FOMO. Do not try to ride a move already started regardless of how much momentum. 

- Wait for the pullback back to a Key Level you trust and from there observe whst direction the market takes in a “most conservative” type of confirmation especially if you trade in 1 mjn TF where lot of false signals are occuring. 

- Avoid taking an entry at or near HOD. Obvious.

author: 305 Trader
content:
hi Adil. I would try avoiding entering at LOD if this was LOD?? altogether or nearby. You can always expect a pullback there which in this case its length was luckily into your marked FVG. How did you determine your reward? Green vs red looks it was just a 1:1.4 RvR. This could be an elevated risk to take. 
Its always advisable to wait for the pullback from HOD or LOD and then watch the reaction at the end of it. Also is the top of the pullback respecting the previous Lower High ? In this case it did. So this is why it worked for you.

author: 305 Trader
content:
good morning. Ok good graphics, great work of journaling. 
Couple of things. Trading before 10 am requires extra caution on false signals given by 1 min TF. The volatility is high in the 1st 30 mins when trading the magnificient 7 such as this one. So, if you see an adverse candle that takes out two large ones of your color right before your set up, just wait. Observe more. In this case the Strong swing low of the market open was respected and then you got your 1st higher low. See the circles in yellow. Entry for me would have been where I marked it.

author: Kash
content: So today's trade in pltr was really good. I see the thesis in the trade, however you just needed to give it a bit more room as you can see the trade worked out. You were unlucky that you cut the trade just before it worked. For me personally PLTR was is a HTF key support level, so I was not confident to short it there. Yesterday's appl trade was similar to day. You were in the right side, however the timing was not on your side. If you trade appl, you know the personality of the stock is to move like this. A lot of fakeouts before the actual move as you can see later on in the day the trade did end up working out (*$% rule) after taking out most of the buyers. I was in appl myself yesterday, but was able to scale 30% at day high to cover my risk, which allowed me to have a managed sl. On monday in nvda, you see that buyers stepped up but no real follow through happened. When playing the reclaim I always wait for 5 min reclain or 1 minute immediate retest, or a candle stick pattern to confirm buyers (this can be a second green candle in this case). If I take the 84% based on the 1 min reclaim only, i found myself getting trapped more often than the wins. This is data I have from my journaling

author: Kash
content: One suggestion, I would like to give to you seeing your P/L calendar is to be more selective with trades. You have to think of reasons to not take a trade rather than take it in my opinion. For the next 10 trades try to take only the trades that look the best in the higher time frame and leave the others, you will start to see more green trades. This is one thing I implemented myself in my trading and saw a massive spike in win % . Moreover, with good HTF trade the chances of cathing a higher r:r trade are much more that allows you to be green even with a lower win %. Meanwhile you are trying this, size as much down as you can and journal untill you see things changing. This will help your financial and mental capital both.Hope this helps.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
What could you tweak next time?

1. Pre-define your invalidation before entry (you did this ✅).
2. If price snaps back under the level in the next 1-3 candles → ask:
   - Is the setup still clean? (structure, volume, QQQ correlation)
   - Am I calm enough to re-enter? (stress = red flag)

If yes → second-chance entry at the same or tighter risk is often higher probability (fakeout flushed weak hands).
But only if you’re calm. Stress = skip it.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Bottom line:
You followed your plan → capital safe → edge intact.
Next time, just have the “second-chance rule” ready in your playbook.

author: JY_sg
content: hi guys, i have some questions. recently when i got stop out, i realise my stops losses are easily more expensive than my wins, meaning to say when I was stop out and i respect my trading plan on the SL, i will lose maybe 20-30% of my position easily, but when i win and scale, my wins are sometimes only 20-40% of my position despite scaling for wins and about the win move higher than my stop out. Any advice on this?

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi Pamela, I made two screenshots. 1st one, is what only in that context (not looking at HTF) was a good set up. Here it is

author: 305 Trader
content:
In a downtrend move, you wait for the pullback or price retracement, or mitigation into the nearest Lower High of the move. You did that for your 1st entry, that’s great. But here is the missing piece of the price structure: 

- as the pullback into your POI creates Higher lows, the reaction you wait for from the sellers has to FIRST break the nearest Higher low, then mitigate again and then, you consider an entry. You need the market to demonstrate a ChOCH first. You dont want to be the ChOCH rider, because that would he “hoping”. You dont hope. 

- Also a RvR of 1:2 to your 1st TP I always measure, and unless volume is large and with it momentum I may break this rule.

author: 305 Trader
content: I see a lot of traders, do not think of this rule of price structure, the ChOCH move. maybe is lack of knowledge. So your entries were all incorrect. Only on that purple line confirmation candle a set up finally came, the ChOCH move had occurred and the price was once more falling “orderly” from a Lower High that respected “again” the Lower High that formed in the 8th bar from market’s open.

author: 305 Trader
content: Whats ChOCH, you can also call it BOS, its a technicality the difference, but what is important is to grasp this rule.

hey Dane, I didn't take this but was watching it (short side is not my best side to put it mildly lol)

author: Kam

Hey Porter - appreciate you laying out your thought process and sorry for the late reply. 

I don’t personally trade with fibs or FVGs in my system, so I’ll keep feedback focused on structure rather than specific tools.

It looks like you did several things well here:

- Waited for a pullback instead of chasing breakdowns
- Had clear targets and didn’t hold blindly

To me, those are solid trading fundamentals in any system.

If I had to give one area to maybe focus on & keep refining, it would be to try to separate directional conviction (“I think it dumps today”) from the actual structure that confirms the entry.

But overall - nice trades man! It seems like you executed your plan which to me is what matters. Keep drilling the process!

author: Ghost
content: Hey everyone! I was just thinking through my approach to trading and was wondering if maybe a more experinced trader could hopefully help me work out the logic. Currently I've been having a decent month, I use 500 risk per trade (calculated by num_cons = 5/drawdown on option con until trade invalidated) and I obviously just have SL at the point of invalidation. Now here is where I am stuck, this seems to be working well however I saw Jack hit 19k and since I had a basically perfect exit efficiency today I decided to run back what his risk would be and came to the conclusion that he does not let his trades run to his actual SL most of the time (since his losers are so small). I was wondering how I should square this with the conventional logic of "not cutting trades early", is this just something that comes with more experience or should I just exercise judgement and cut trades when they don't immediately go in my favor? Any advice helps!

author: 305 Trader
content:
Good observation. I have also observed that Jacks SL are pretty wide often. I do not plug in in the live trading. What I suggest is, focus on what is working for you. Look for set ups with 1:3 RvR that actually have a small stop loss, one you can afford and be comfortable with especially because it can be hit.  One that will not put you in disarray and cause to go crazy about the loss. Because a loss is perhaps more certain than a win in this business. 
Jack trades big league and his tolerance for a stop loss (mentally and account wise), is not the same as yours and mine.

author: Das Wookie
content:
<@1216811466028613744> was asking about my entry, and I got a few PMs about my trade, so figured I'd open a discussion here if anybody wanted to chit-chat further about it.

I catch SOOO much s*** for using a 1 second Timeframe, and my use of the Heikin-Ashi candles.... So let me state I ONLY use HA for the 1 second TF. HA will delay your view by 1 candle, so sure, I'm 1 second behind, but it works for me. I'm not SPLIT second trading anyway, but I do like the view into the 1 minute candle intra-minute that doesn't require me to have to further interogate the candle for exactly what's happening. I generally keep the 1 second TF zoomed out to show me minimum 3 minutes, but usually closer to 5 minutes. I also have charts open for 1 minute and 5 minute, as well as L2 and Time & Sales data up on another screen.

author: Das Wookie
content: Not only did I enter on the initial pullback from 9:30:48 to 9:31:33, I also entered based on the fact that I was also looking at HUGE numbers in the ASK side of L2 and L2 BID numbers for size were also at that time significantly smaller. The L2 data showed large ASK limit orders all that way up to ~$294. I figured this was probably for people still trying to unload short positions from the prior day, so I didn't know if it would be a quick scalp, but I clearly saw at least a $1.50 move so, I went in w/100 contracts. I still didn't have a good feeling for what the larger theme of the day would be. It could possibly have a pullback after the initial open spike, or if it could continue to run... but as soon as it started to run I just kept my eye on the L2, Time & Sales, and price action. At that first real pullback at $297.50 the L2 was pretty flat, but QQQ still showed movement... so I just put my faith in the Q's and let it ride... while still holding my finger over the button to close the entire position. When it started to rebound, L2 showed the way, plus at that point it looked like a 1 candle rule entry, and Q's still looked OK so I continued to let the entire position ride. At the point I sold, I was looking at the ATH established last month, and when the price bobbled, I scaled out 80% and setup the sell for the remaining 20% if it ended up turning... holding my breath... as it went into consolidation. I had internally set my SL at $299, with finger on the trigger... and then... recovery and climbing. In the $302-$304 ranging, I didn't know if it was consolidation and futher climbing, or if it was done. It was trailers, and I was hoping to get over the $60k point for the trade, so I held... then it reversed and started to drop, and I closed out the final 20%. One hell of a ride! 😄

author: Das Wookie
content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

Generally, yeah, I'm striving for max profits, but...

I'll scale out if it's a scalp trade and I'm not looking to play it out past a pullback. I'll also level out if I hit a key level if I'm in it for longer and am willing to sit through a pullback... sometimes I'll scale out some, other times I'll hold but be ready to VERY quickly dump the trade if it looks like a reversal. Some of it's just based on my thesis for the trade and if I have a reason to believe that the trade hasn't played out yet. Sometimes that means I end up making less because I might have leveled out earlier or exited the trade.

There's so many factors I'm considering at that exact moment I would be incapable of even enumerating them all... Basically tho, I'm boiling down to one of three thoughts:
- SCRAM the trade! It's reversed! GET OUT! NOW!!!
- Scale out most of our profits / hold some trailers, because the magic 8-Ball says: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
- We're Too Close. Stay on Target. Lighten up! Stay On Target!!!

author: Das Wookie
content:
I have made my own rules, and a lot of my Options Trading rules differs from my Shares Trading rules. This is because with shares, you can still buy when volume is only 3,000 a minute. You can also trade pretty much everything, where as with options... No Effin Way!!! The 11am rule is there for a liqudity and volume reason, but if both are still there, party on. In GENERAL tho, once you are into intraday trading, it gets SIGNIFICANTLY harder! Today was a fluke.... and a 22-point drop in the Q's was a VERY target rich environment. GOOG and PLTR was where I went, but you could have just as easily gone with SQQQ, AMD, AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, AMZN, NVDA, NFLX, META, ORCL, MU, MSTR, AVGO, INTC, COIN, CRWV, HOOD, OKLO, RKLB, SOFI, TSM... everything was in freefall. Really, gains this afternoon was pretty much limited only by ones capital and bandwidth to throw at the trades. I can't personally manage more than 2 trades at a time. Well maybe if I threw a follow stop at it, and gave myself something like a $1 pullback, but I just can't do that... even tho that would have been REDICULOUSLY profitable to have done today. At one point, I did finally convince myself "Just throw a trade at it on your other brokerage account!" for my swing and hold account, because I was essentially monitoring the exact same trade. I really wish I would have done that for more but... coulda woulda shoulda would have ever one of us become billionaires! 

As for 11a... that's there also to protect ME from MYSELF with chasing more profits into the intraday trading, and likely giving up my morning gains. FAR too often have I gone spelunking for more profits, only to give up my entire mornings gains. So, unless I've got an A+ setup... and I'm just unable to resist entering... I let it go. Today was a GOOOOOOD reason to break the no trades after 11a rule!

author: Das Wookie
content:
I generally am not, no. Sometimes I'll have it on in the background, just so I can listen in on what the boys are doing, but for the most part I'm doing my own thing. I trade more than B&R, and watch more symbols... so if the Mag 7 aren't doing anything interesting, and my thesis for the day ended up wrong... well then I'll just go wherever my watchlist leads me... and where one of my setups appears.

Today, it would have been VERY difficult to have not printed if you were still paying attention after 11, and the bottom of the bucket fell out for the next 2 hours. Today was an amazing velocity trade to the downside!!! This sorta day doesn't come around very often... at least not without some tweet from the pumpkin king that causes the entire market to panic. I was in a $40k trade last time that happened, and my jaw hit the floor!!! "Holy ****!!!! What the... What just happened!??? Babe! Pull up the news. Did the pentagon just explode or a dirty bomb go off in NY or LA????" as I watched TRILLIONS of dollars vaporize before our very eyes. I didn't have the emotional stability to cut my loss RIGHT THEN and switch to PUTS. I was too locked in on my CALL trade, and didn't want to cut the position... thinking "This HAS to come back! This is insane! What is the market doing?!!! C'Mon!!! Come back up! Please! PLEASE!!!" where as now, I'd just cut it. RIGHT then. Without Mercy! "It's just numbers." and these days, I'd just SCRAM the trade. That's also the basis for some of my rules. We all establish our own rules. Sometimes I break mine, but I do my level best to only do it for a VERY good reason. Sometimes tho, I slip up... like with my two RGTI trades today which was the basis of violating more than half of the rules I broke today. Of course, had I NOT broken that rule, and tried to chase a win there... I'd have already been out of the market and entirely missed this afternoons drop because I'd have been out of the market already and on to do other things.

author: Das Wookie
content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. Instead, I chased, and entered a trade without a proper setup entry JUST because it looked like it might be good. I sized WAY up to 400 contracts too try and make up for my loss. Turns out tho, there were NO options traders on that symbol. The Bid/Ask Spread was MASSIVE with a $3.50 spread, but I didn't notice until I went to exit. There were NO buyers. NONE!!! I pulled up the Time & Sales, and I thought the data connection was broken. I pulled up history, and I saw some trades of mostly 1 contact, a few for 2 contracts, a rare 5 or 10, and then my 400 contacts buy. There we no other options trades. I couldn't exit! Well I could, but with THAT spread, it was BRUTAL. I listed the limit sale at the ASK, and nothing happened for 30 seconds. I dropped my price by $0.10. Then again. And again. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

author: Das Wookie
content: I can't believe I did this, but I did. I've never been trapped by such a unbelievably massive spread before! I did EVERYTHING wrong today.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I try to be up most of the time as well with my outlook. Yeah, I'm still green for the week, and it was one week I'll remember for sure! I shouldn't have traded today. I felt self applied pressure to pull a big win again today.

The market wasn't having it tho.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Also I wanted to point this out for you incase you missed it. I am not sure exactly what you were running your risk off of here, but if you zoom out to the 5M we just had a down close candle in an uptrend that retest and held ORH. This also coincides with the previous high on the 1M. Here, your risk is only extended by about 3 points, and has a proper point of invalidation to break below which was never wicked. I put the 1M and 5M beside each other here so you could see what that looks like in real time. I understand you run hard bracket stops and doing so, wicks will tap you out of a lot of trades so its always best to have a true and proper point of invalidation.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Running hard stops is completely fine just always be sure to be aware of what the other time frames are shaping up like compared to the one you are trading on, and have a proper and defined risk. Jack and Tony emphasize the importance of a candle close so eventually you will get the grasp after enough wick outs then price running straight to TP, you will be able to identify how to avoid it in the future. I have saved my myself from fake invalidations and had very good trades play out by not letting a wick stop me, but I’ve also had some trades seriously run away by waiting for candle close. So It has its pros and cons and eventually, based on your own trading style, you will find out what works best for you and a lot of times it is based on situational context. But in the meantime I do strongly recommend a proper and defined risk level if you are running hard stops. I used to trade the 1M as well, but now I only enter and scale on it. My risk and target is always managed and defined on the 5 and 15 and its taken a lot of the unnecessary losses out, stopped me from entering fakeout/sweeps, and improved my win rate and trading overall.

author: DWalt Trades
content: I recommend doing trade review videos on all your trades, bar by bar in replay mode, considering more than one time frame, everyday after session. Along with mass backtesting sessions that are recorded. This will help you identify how and where to cut down the overtrading and hold your trades until they either are invalidated or objective filled. I say this because technically your first entry @ 25,798.50 was never invalidated, and your initial trade could’ve been held to main objectives instead of having multiple smaller trades inside of the initial main position. This could have been a one and done day. I used to find myself in trade reviews saying “I should have just held my initial entry” on a breakeven or small red day that could’ve very well been green days and ultimately those deep reviews are where my improvement came from. That is the main reason I responded to your first text in here about it because I immediately identified something that I used to fall victim to as well on your chart so any questions just tag me here and I am happy to help!

author: DWalt Trades
content:
Hi 🙂

I can 100% totally see where you are coming for sure because I used to think the same thing until I saw the change that it made so as someone that now does it I can’t say I agree with you! It seems hard such as all good habit creation does until.. eventually it’s just a habit. Then you realize it was just a self-limiting belief. Yes I may have to put a few extra hours in overnight. but the TRUE reality is that this is not going to be sweet as a cake. We trade NY MKT OPEN 9:30-11:00 which is not even a full 2 hours. Nobody’s situation will change only working 1.5 hours a day! Those “extra hours” are where success derives from. This may not be my biggest year in the market Profit wise due to a lower trade frequency & size, but it is by far my biggest year win rate & growth wise — so to me, my best year in the market — So yes maybe I put in an extra 3 hours at night - sometimes even a LOT more. But It’s worth it for the clarity. It’s worth it to be more comprehensive in the market. It’s worth it to see a losing setup repeat itself and stay hands off and then vice versa for winners. It`s worth it to go from a get-rich mindset to a longevity based mindset. It’s almost like double experience IMO but this is definitely a to each his own situation. The solution to anyone’s problems in trading will be found this way though, is why I recommended it.

I’ve dropped a few key points of how much data I’ve tracked since just APRIL 2025 bc thats what I can account for sure (where i got organized) It used to be exhausting. Eventually I found a rhythm with my daily schedule to where now I look forward to it. Just wanted to show that even as a student it’s possible — & not as hard as it seems — & give you some insights on why I think it’s important. So overall In hindsight — Im a completely different trader now and honestly It doesn’t require much but a little extra work… Which I see all of our coaches put in every single day & I strive to be as successful as them.

author: Patty
content:
There are few thing that can be corrected in this trade:
. If you mark the 5 minute high with a line, you will figure out that this was an immediate retest and it is advisable to take those with displacement.
. You entered on the confirmation candle which is good but that was already at high of the day which is first take profit for most of us… So if the risk to reward does not makes sense, trades like this can be avoided also..
. For the stop placement, it should be on the bottom of retesting candle which in other words is the market structure as well..

content: The retest which happened around 10:12 was a nice one with displacement and good risk to reward at your first take profit which you got easily…

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jason - a few thoughts, hope it helps!

1) QQQ inside PM range - META RS but not correlated with indices. low-vol holiday week and big opening drive, probably need a strong catalyst to sustain momo

2) PDH Immediate retest technically valid but super aggressive given context

3) I like the 2nd entry but don't see this as 84% rule since 1-candle structure never failed. More of a PDH + 1-candle reclaim play. When didnt hit HOD + made lower high + failed the PDH retest I would have probably cut this aggressively given overall context.

author: Jason
content: Thanks for the feedback, how do you know when RS isn’t correlated to indices? Is it just knowing how the stock moves? When would you need aggressive when the stock and indices diverge? Probably not on low volume week…

author: Kam
content:
I try to think about it this way: when your ticker and the indices are outside ranges & moving in the same direction you have correlation. If direction / trend is aligned AND your ticker is leading the move, then you have real RS. 

If indices are inside range + chopping but your ticker is leading, you have RS but no correlation - that was the situation this morning with META. That's when you need to know a ticker's character to know if it can decouple / move independently, especially without a catalyst

author: Huy
content:
I am going to share this since it may help a few people out because I'm sure I am not the only one feeling like this. Trading all comes down to a probability game. 
Jack called the AMZN play yesterday - i took it and it was a loser
he then called the INTC play. He profited but I didn't take it because I was already at a loss and didn't want to hop into another trade just because.

fast forward to today, its the same thing again lol. He called the TSLA put. I took a bigger lost than i'd like because of the impulsive green candle at 9:47am (+$1.99!). He then took the NVDA put and profited. I didn't enter for the same reason as yesterday.

I ended today in a loss again BUT i must say; i managed my risk the best that I could and didn't have an itch to enter another trade. I am done for the day. This is how we will win in the long run. If you feel down - go do something. Punch a bag. Go to the gym and lift some weights. Go for a walk or run then review the trades and why it didn't work out. Today was a win in my books. I didn't make the dollar gains but I made some mental gains 💪 

p.s. I only entered their live trades because I see what Jack and Tony see and have the same thesis. No entering in blindly.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok. Mike Y, I think you may be trading in a complicated system, though if it works for you keep doing it. Now, the brackets Limit orders may not be suitable for options because, if you are right the top limit order can limit you as the options contract value grows exponentially once it hits ITM. On the other hand, a stop limit order to a loss can be 15 cts in the first go but then it may go down to 30 cts on the 2nd retracement while the underlying is the same $ value. So take a look at this. I think is a better way to manage risk when you know what is your max loss per trade and per day and adjust accordingly your contract size AND, filter out the RvR more suitable. This latter will keep you away from trading what does not meet your trading plan.

author: Humantorch
content: What's the minimum con amount where it starts to make sense to have trailers beyond your initial tp1/2?

author: Humantorch
content: sounds good,  waiting for your response when you're back 🙂

author: Huy
content:
Seems like the average recommended position size to scale out is around 80% between TP 1 and 2. A few folks here would recommend only leaving a trailer if you have more than 5-6 cons but I personally think you can start with 3 for A+ set ups. Sell the first 2 cons early and leave the last one to see if it can ride the wave. The only downside is if the trade go against you then your gains from the first 2 cons will be wiped. Thats why I think 5 cons works better since you can sell 4 early then leave 1 to run. That 1 does not have enough weight to pull the prior profits if managed correctly. 

I am a big believer in actually doing. We can backtest / do simulation work / pretend how we scale in our minds etc but everything changes when you have real contracts and the tape is live. 

I just started to use this sheet here to help me quickly set my TP in the bracket order. The upside is I can lock in small consistent gains. The downside is I will miss on bigger moves on the first 80% of my position size. You can adjust the ATR to be more aggressive if you like which will change the values a bit.

let me know if you have any feedbacks!

author: Humantorch
content:
i dont take ATR into account so it's hard to comment on that aspect.
With 5 cons it's doable, but to be on safe side you would probably do 3-1-1 (3 HOD which is >50% of position then 1 con at TP1 and TP2) but depending on where the Key Levels are, and how you trade, may not be enough to be called a trailer position. 
With 6 cons, it starts to look better. 3 HOD(50%) then 1-1-1 if you want to catch bigger moves or 2-1 for safer approach.
WIth 7 cons i think this is where it starts to shine. 4HOD to have >50% of position secured then either 1-1-1 or 2-1. 
I feel like 7 is the ideal minimum, with 6 being a starter of what's possible. 
what do you think?

author: Huy
content:
I know more than a handful of people were affected by the NVDA trade today. Here's a couple of thoughts;

1. NVDA likes to do this recently so we have to be aggressive in scaling out
2. Do not wait for Jack or Tony to say that they have entered. It might already be too late by then for quicker scalpes with little to no continuation.
3. This is the very reason why I always do a bracket order to take profit a portion of my contracts when we get that first pop. Our fingers can't ever be as fast.

I'm sure a few you guy saw how AMD and MU had those BANGERS continuation last week so y'all had the same expectation today. This can altered the way we manage our current trades. Journal journal journal! I'd like to hear any honest words or thoughts. author: DSmith
content: Hey everyone - I’m really looking for some recommendations going into next month and to get my mind right/refreshed.  I’ve been through the courses and joining the Live sessions since mid June of last year. I’m watching all of the reviews and YouTube videos. Each month it seems like I’ll be really improving and seeing consistent wins, and then I end up giving it all back and more on a few trades, (or like this past week for example) and pretty much wasted a whole month’s worth of progress…start over again and then it happens the next month and so on. 🙃 Last week I realized that I really needed to try and not scale so quickly/let trades play out longer with my small 1-3 cons because my winners were so much smaller than my losers when I scale at HOD. Tried that approach this week and obviously wasn’t a smart move…out of all weeks this one was the one where I should’ve just continued to scale at HOD. $4500 acct turned into $3400 in a week…and I’m definitely feeling a bit hesitant going into the next month if I stay on this trajectory. What have you guys done to get out of this situation and survive another day? Is the answer to scale? Or not to scale? 😂 I mean on MUs and SNDKs I’m basically just on 1 con, Fridays I’ve been downsizing, and then still get wrecked! I liked the setup that Jack took today on QQQ puts…actually got in like right before he did, and then the sucker just had the most massive green candle right after the entry…🤦. Maybe the lesson is just to completely avoid FOMC and earnings weeks all around. Here’s my month- AAPL on the 27th which was an amazing trade but all I had was -0.32 cents to show for it!😂 I appreciate any and all feedback!!

author: DSmith

Entries and exits may be a bit late here and there but I find that on good trades I scale majority out on HOD and just don’t get a lot of extra gas on 1 con, or maybe I take it off at TP1. It’s just the Stop Losses, every time I lose it’s usually like 1 or 2 huge red candles that just kill me and I stop out on strong weakness with market orders…often I check back later in the day just to find that the stock went up later in the day etc. and I probably would’ve made huge profits if I didn’t get faked out. But losing on those 3 cons with such weakness just eats away my small profits that I take on 1 or 2 at HOD and then 1 con left for TP1.

author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: John H
content: One final thought. I would like to thank <@801169196015943731> for the amount of effort he puts into this discord. The daily premarket prep, the staying a few minutes after each trading session to answer questions. The detailed trade reviews win or lose. The time he dedicates to us on Saturdays to review trades and answer questions. This is a true coach & mentor we are lucky to have. I have not tuned in to a Saturday session in a while, and something today was telling me I should. I heard a lot of things I needed to hear again about trade management, sizing risk, and having a gameplan. Appreciate you Jack!

author: Fred
content:
Hi, here my trade for today. The result wasn't very good : two losses (-2.7R) and one win (+2R).
What's your opinion on the process ?

In 1h TF, we broke and retested the previous high from last Wednesday. Last Thursday, we broke the Wednesday low, which we then retested on Friday. The plan today was either a continuation of the bearish trend (break of the red zone/PDL) or a rejection of the previous low/PDH (red zone).

In 15m TF I identified 2 potential areas for a reverse (green area, which coincides with the red zone for rejection in 1h TF) and 1 area for continuation (red area).

On the 1m TF, the scenario is bullish; we disregard the bearish continuation opportunity and focus on the reversal. Based on my key zones identified on the 1-hour and 15-minute charts, I took puts on breakouts and retested previous 1-minute highs (market structure/yellow area).

author: Luke
content: Hey <@293563891345129472>, I really like the trade based on a "5min setup." As far as momentum on the 1min timeframe, you would like to have seen it work out right away rather than consolidating. After about 10:12am EST, I would have not been interested in it anymore based on the 1min timeframe since you would like to see it reject immediately especially for shorts. This is where you could go to the 5min timeframe if you have more of a bearish thesis and look to enter based off of the 5min timeframe. Also, another reason this would be a "5min setup" (you'll hear Jack and Tony refer to these as that) is you can see that there is a lot of displacement... in my opinion just a little too much for momentum shorts on the 1min timeframe for me, but based on the 5min chart it looks super nice. But besides that, your stop loss looks solid and your entry. This was definitely a trade you could have looked to re-enter with the 84% Rule on. Nice trade though!!

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content:
I took a quick apple scalp since JDUB was looking at it and bought two 0 day cons. One sold at profit of 17 and one tight stop out at -9. They reversed shortly after so my exit was ok. Then I saw NVDA come back within 5 bucks of where I entered. It retested and I tried again with Jack and chat talking about continuation (but not high conviction on retrospect, it didn’t close under the level I was looking at with conviction and the market reversed). While I was wrong on the setup I did manage risk by switching from 0 days to next Friday expiry. I had a tighter stop but sized up like an idiot to 3 cons instead of sticking to 1 on a lower probability setup and overall should have stayed out because it is Friday and my 2nd attempt at NVDA already failed. Looking back it felt rational but was an emotional trade. I lost another 118 in my margin account stopping out. 

I took a solo trade going long on QQQ when it popped above the 15 minute orb and defended it with the 10:31 candle, it did not continue higher so I sold my single 0 day con for a profit of $33.00 very quickly. This turned out to be a smart decision as it didn’t break higher for two more hours, by then most would have stopped out and any contract would have decayed.

I gave spy long a try but had an ultra tight stop and was done at -9 bucks, also wise as it rejected that level about 10 times and then failed bigly hours later.

author: Joebag009
content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

author: Joebag009
content:
Jack and Tony signed off for the day so I switched my attention to FlowZone on Youtube my Take Profit Trader (TPT) and Topstep evals with mnq micros. Lots of trades where take profit and stop were within a range of 20 bucks to get out of the trade as the scalp setup failed. On TPT my eval is almost blown from being on tilt the other day, I went from 248 of wiggle room down to 104 so I paused on that account and hope to trade back on an A+ setup later or on a different day. I was careful and followed my rules, just had the trade go against me. I tried one more trade later and was wrong, down to 86 in the eval, maybe I’ll get lucky next week and build it back up. If I blow it I will focus on my other evals. I tried a few props and the number I have now is too much to trade. My favorite overall is TPT-reasonable cost, love tradeovate online, not as rule heavy as Apex.

On topstep I ground to +150 then down to 50 then ranged 60 to 120 and took a pause as the market just couldn’t decide what it wanted and scalping volatility died down and I wanted to protect my hard won gains. I then broke my rule of being done when the streamer is and went on tilt, shut off the chart at -283 so I can live to fight next week.

author: Joebag009
content:
I keep getting to the half way point of passing evals and then getting close to blowing them, grinding up, then either failing with tight margin or a second tilt day. I wish I had tried props sooner, days of learning only costs me the eval fee. I wish more that I was passing them. I want to try trading multiple accounts at the same time with a copy trader for base hits that add up, I have two Apex accounts and wanted to link them to Ninja trader, only to find out that the feature was removed with the latest update to Ninja 8. I might try trading both together on Rthmic trader pro, but haven’t figured out the right settings and also need to tweak the master settings to switch from Ninja to Rthmic. I was super exited for Ninja with all the flashy Youtube shorts out there, it looks like ass out of the box and I am let down. So far my best results have been with TPT. Apex trailing drawdown scares me, I need 1 solid trade per day which might be good for my trading, but if I trail too close I’ll get stopped out in profit and then if I try to re-enter I’ll get cooked on small moves and spreads. If I size up I can’t give it too much room without blowing the account. Two micros and one trade attempt per day might be the best way to go, but how much is a healthy stop and healthy trail for Apex. I think Flowzone has a video on Apex best trading practice so I’ll check it out. I need to start trading the accounts next week so I can pass before they renew but this week has sucked and I’m not starting on a Friday.

Between all my accounts my challenges are: overtrading, tilt, sizing up instead of being consistent, not differentiating between good and crap setups, and rule breaking by staying on charts too long.

author: Joebag009
content: Wednesday I reviewed again all my Feb trades. I would be green in all my accounts this month if I just followed my rules. This means I need to shift focus from working on my trades and rules to working on myself. This would be a good time to mention my adhd brain makes trading an emotional roller coaster, even with meds and living with the diagnosis over a decade now. My goal today was to shut off the computer when streamers were done (no solo trades anymore until I get my mojo back as in profitable again). I broke it and it hurt me again, so I will do more inner work this weekend to figure out how my rational brain keeps getting circumvented even with rules and repeated failure.

author: Luke
content:
Hey <@708108045301055548>, I see what you were going for here. I see a few things here: I like that 5min low for a move down to HTF key levels. It looks like you entered at 10:06am EST on the reclaim of Premarket Low. Before that though, you can see that I have a level from the 15min timeframe (not a super strong level but good for context). You can see it actually swept it beforehand and bounced off of it, so for me I would need to wait for a reclaim or retest of the 179.45; it looks like you didn't have that key level drawn so in your case the the context is pretty good.
One other small thing you can see is there was a lower-high made at 9:57am EST and it was actually broken at 10:03am EST and the 1min timeframe technically had already reversed before you entered, and was now creating a higher-low.
Those are just some small things that stick out to me. Overall solid trade idea though. You can see on the HTF that NVDA has a lot of room down if it can get below Premarket Low/1790.45 HTF key level and has room down to at least Feb 6th's LOD. Hope that helps

author: Joebag009
content:
Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

TopStep: no trades.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Joebag009
content:
Big aha with FZ today is that just like with JT I overtraded and didn’t quite match his execution. My goal today was to trade less and more focused on his stops and setups. While I missed some of the moves and botched the execution a bit, I at least paid attention to him with a new type of mindset and followed through and am up in 1 out of two Apex accounts. I could feel my frontal cortex straining, doing REPS, as I breathed through my emotions and focused on market structure. I stayed OUT of MULTIPLE trades, that WOULD NOT HAVE WORKED. So that was a huge psych win for me today, which I need cause I feel like I’m on the cusp of either making it big or completely losing my mind. FZ did 3 trades today, I did more with less size, same pattern as with JT and re-entries, but a bit easier managed on the charts so less friction. 

12:30 PM Eastern behaviour of futures seems to change a bit and most consistent pros are done here if not a lot sooner. Big AHA: a price move on a chart is not equal, cons and futures move different depending on price volume and market structure. “d’uh” but seeing it in real time is different than seeing it in a lesson. A “choppy/grindy” move in both futures and contracts is a lot crappier than a clean smaller move with strong volume and direction. Again “d’uh”, but starting to see it is making a big difference in my emotions and execution. Also after 12:30 fills get worse on stopping out and taking profits making it harder to stack up wins that recover from even small losses (because bad fills make them even 3x worse than AM exit with the same distance stop). In other words, what the chart shows is a lie, the risks of extracting money from what you see go UP.

author: Joebag009
content:
Low of day to VWAP is a great secondary setup for futures, not usually for options as they move differently. Maybe I could capture that move on an index weekly, but I’m usually busy trading futures on that move. Important to note that into lunch and beyond sudden reversals from nowhere more likely, so hard stops needed on futures and not options friendly. 

You don’t need a strong reason to stay out, gut feeling is enough in addition to technicals, but you sure as shit need a strong reason to enter.

Old me got a reward from taking a trade as well as winning, new me is trying to stay neutral and get a reward from following rules. Trader vs gambler.

SORRY THESE POSTS ARE SO LONG... I'm not doing great right now, I will try to shorten these as I work through the challenges.

author: Porter
content:
I should have mentioned this earlier, but I did **not** intend to use the 5-minute timeframe for the entry not on this trade itself.

* The trade was meant to be a **break and retest of the pre-market level (PML)**.
* The **5-minute level** was only being used as a **price target** in this situation.
* At the time, the ticker was **below both the PML and the PDL**, with **very bearish price action** from the prior day.
* Because of that, I felt using the **PML as resistance** made sense.
* Price had **wicked into the EMA and VWAP twice and rejected**, which reinforced that bias.

I should also clarify:

* I trade primarily on the **3-minute chart**, not the 1-minute.
* That said, after dropping down to the **1-minute**, I see what you mean about the entry — and you’re right.

Looking back at it now using **TradingView replay**:

* The **9:36 candle on the 3-minute** was the correct entry.
* I entered **too late**, mainly due to hesitation on my part.
* There also wasn’t any real **displacement** at the point where I entered.

Honestly, the only way this trade would’ve worked — now that I think about it — was:

* Entering on the **second retest of the wick during the formation of the 9:36 candle**.

One question for you:

* When price is **below both the PML and the PDL**, do you **prioritize the 5-minute levels**, or is that **circumstantial** for you as well?
* And you’re absolutely right about **shorting at the key level**.

Sorry — ChatGPT helped me write this 😅

---

author: Huy
content:
first candle rule (5 mins opening orb range) is something that is always good to map out and see so you can build some context. If the price is below both the PDL and PML, that would be one confluence for me. I like to take key levels into consideration such as the HTF levels. I am going to steal Jack's words but WMT offered that beautiful pop and fade today. 
PDL + 128.10 key support would have been a low risk entry. PML and PMH should be used with caution though since the levels created in this session does not have a lot of volume so its a thing we can look at if there's a HTF thesis and PM levels is the only thing that we can work off in a *trending* day.

if presented with this trade, I would only take a short off the 128.10 level that I mentioned either or go long if it can break and retest above 129.70 since this would violate the current structure and can shift for the upside. 

p.s. if you trade with wmt more in the future, you can watch the XLP index for another confluence

author: Kam
content:
I’d probably look at the 9:42 candle after the hammer at the 5m OR as the best retest. It’s aggressive (ahead of the 1m OB), but it has confluence with a prior daily level from the prior Fri high and is inline with the 1st pullback method

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

IMO the 2nd best momentum retest came later around 11:20 on the 5m once relative strength was obvious.

Big thing though is replaying & practicing these moments in real-time speed. In hindsight a lot of things can look textbook, but we have to go with what we can see in the moment! Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

i see what you were trying to do. Good attempt but here's the issues. You did the right thing by waiting for good price action but 9:45am est candle had a major wick which means sellers really brought it down. 
418 is a pivot point so the idea is;
- we need to see a candle pushing past the 9:45am candle
- the above will in turn go past 418
- this is still tricky since we are walking into an order block from 9:33am

in conditions like this, a closure above 418 would be a good confirmation to potentially go long

author: Huy
content:
As many of you have already agreed on this - no more 0's! No ifs. The trade has to work out right away and the move has to be impulsive for 0's to work in our favor. Also it takes another level of mental capital to be able to hold through the ups and downs. Only Jack and Tony can get away with this due to where they are in their trading journey + their experience. My suggestion is only take 0's once you get to a certain point but the size is going to be much smaller. Vast majority of us including myself are not there yet.

I can give you 2 feedbacks on the trade today. 
1. NVDA was consolidating after we got in but it never broke market structure. There was an order block that it was respecting nicely
2. QQQ also never broke structure

do not get me wrong, today was also tough. It takes some mental fortitude to hold through that

content: Are you comparing how the SL should be for let’s say 1 DTE vs 4?

author: Annie Nguyen
content: Yes bc with 0dte we give it a tighter SL, but for trade 2dte or 4dte idk how we calculate our SL

author: Huy
content: The sliding scale is your SL is closer to the 10-20%  mark the further your expiration is. The closer the expiration the wider your SL % is. Some people still give 20-40% for 1 DTE. But really, focus on market sound structure and pick a later expiration if possible. This will allow you to mentally stay in the position longer.

$180 calls went from around $4.15 to $4.83 (about a 16–17% move). author: FraggDieb
content:
First, I did not watch his review video. I guess he is explaining that. But yes Tony has a position size way over 100 contracts. So 50k position size sounds correct. 

Yes with 0dte you CAN make much more compared to the weekly’s. But there is no better risk/reward. there is only much more risk (for more reward for sure) because when it’s going flat or against you, your position is so fast at -30% or more.

author: Kam
content:
Hey there, that’s a really self-aware recap. I think being able to clearly explain what you saw and where it went wrong is a good sign you’re processing things the right way.

Most of us have had plenty of days where the read was right but the execution wasn’t, that’s a frustrating but very normal phase of the learning curve and just a part of the profession. If you follow Jack and Tony live or even catch the tape after the fact, you'll see plenty of days where even they have this frustration. 

Taking a short reset is usually a good move. When you come back, just focus on the process again and keep the size small while you refine the entries.

author: Joebag009
content: Hello friends, a few of you have been so kind to engage with me and you might remember I have not been doing well both in trading and personally. I haven't posted in a while so here is an update: I have kept journalling in crazy detail the technicals, the feelings before duirng and after the trade, pages each day and often on paper so it was just too much to share on here daily. I am trying to improve my consistency, avoid losses, and follow rules - trading really is you vs you and a journey. I slowed down my losses to basically breakeven since about mid February, but still not reverted to steady profitability like I hoped. The market has been difficult these past weeks as you all know, also a host of bad luck for me: internet outages at terrible times that stop out otherwise banger trades, broker glitches, and off the screen for personal matters and funerals on almost all the continuation days. Yesterday I got stopped out by 1-5 ticks multiple times... literally 2 points into my stop and then the market did that 200+ point pump, and stopped me on an attemt to re-enter on the pullback by a couple points. I am grinding away some evals on props and fighting with my broker. They admitted an outage and it cost me about $1000 and hope I can recover more than a small credit they offered. I am done with that broker now and will come back to options after I switch. IKBR has just not been great for me, but I'll re-watch Jack and Tony's content on it. I'm also considering CMC markets. Any recommendations? Must work for Alberta, Canada and bonus if it integrates with Ninja Trader. I keep watching the streams daily to stay in touch with everything and hope to trade again soon. Without getting into detail, I worked my way through two degrees, had a 6 figure job, and now my life is in shambles. If you are doing well treasure it and sock away some cash for a rainy day, without my emergency funds I would be living in a cardboard box right now.

author: 305 Trader
content: one of the secrets of staying alive as an Options Trader, is to lose small. So I share with you a trick from observation. When you take an entry and the stock moves in your direction but wicks out a lot, this means there is still indecision. Do not get caught in that fight. Exit at small green or small red and wait for conviction from your Buyers. You can always reenter, if you were correct the market will go the way you needed and if it doesnt you were out already.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Henry - <@1325884177291280475> made a great video on this subject, definitely check it out - highly recommend it. 

Short story is, taking profit on smaller size / account usually requires some adjustment in your profit taking approach in order to make the R-multiple math work https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222375088318447637/1462940540986458323

author: 305 Trader
content: ok out of the money, just right below At the money is best. Then 2nd is your RvR consideration. Reftain from taking a trade les than 1:3 especially when learning when to enter and when to exit. 1:3 is to your ultimate but realistic take profit zone, and, make sure your initial RvR to HOD or LOD is 1:1. This is so you have less of a gamble situation and more of a high prob trade. This is also taught by <@1325884177291280475>

author: 305 Trader
content: so from your entry to HOD or LOD, 1:1 RvR (when applicable), but always as long as your ultimate TP zone is 1:3 from your entry.try to implement this, you will start noticing the progress right away.

Someone told me that Hyenuk the Korean/Colombian trader who teaches in Spanish applies the rule of 8% of his contracts size for everything, either swings or intraday. This is so with slippage and your own reluctance to exit will never become more than 10% of your contract size that loss. People forget that we can always re-enter. Commissions are low, and unless you trade 50% of your account per trade, you can always re-enter. 
Never forget this, you can always re-enter. It took me almost 4 years to understand and apply this simple notion to become profitable. Sometimes, the obvious things are realized late and very very costly.

author: 305 Trader
content: hi no one answered to you, and that’s not nice. I am going to try to give you my insight. Firstly, when taking a pullback or a market correction to the d/s in the indices or stock, make sure the 1H TF is in alignment with that thesis. In a very bullish trending market as it was today or this week, taking a PUT just because your 1 min TF and  the 5 min TF tell you so, can be a trap. Just examine SPY and QQQ today 04-22 and you will agree on what I write.  Price can just pull down to an area of liquidity and then boom bounce right back to continue climbing or just be trapped in a lateral movement. Easiest trades are when you take them with the trend. And if no trend is clear, just wait, do nothing. Check other symbols in your watchlist on the 5 min and 15 min TF.
