author: 305 Trader
content: Then the creamy area for 5 ORB, is helpful, I think a 5 ORB and a 15 min ORB can be useful, though you will notice that the method I described above is reliable and for me is more of a high prob. because is based on identifying points of confluence in the past and recent past and connecting the, on rectangles around a PDsH or PDsL horizontal lines.

author: 305 Trader
content: The more confluence an area has overtime, the more powerful it is since institutions respect them because they created them basically. Whereas, a 5 min or 15 min ORB is only relevant to that day you are trading.

author: 305 Trader
content: Here, this is how I learned it from both Jack and Tony. Now choose three colors only for horizontal lines, in this case there are more because of the ATHs levels we are having, but typically I use:  Red for PDsH (Supply area is usually red), Green for PDsL (demand area is usually green). Tony uses it inverted but I think is more accurate Red = Top = Supply  and Green = Botom = Demand. Then I use a third color to identify a strong zone of multiple points of confluence over time whether on the D chart but mostly on the 1H TF found, and then I proceed to connect those horizontal lines with a relevant area of confluence on a lower TF  for me being the 10 mins TF, but you can use the 15 mins  TF. This is the most important part of your morning routine in preparation for the market opening, identifying where those areas of liquidity are there.

author: JP400
content: Good morning. The black line is the 8 EMA based on the Chart TF (so 1min in this case). I use this in conjunction with the pink line which is the 3 EMA also based on the Chart TF (so 1 min in this case). I want the 3 to be above the 8. If I notice the 3 and 8 constantly crossing, I am hesitant. If the 3 holds above, then I feel more confident that the price will hold in the direction I intend. The orange dotted line is the VWAP, and I want price to be on my side of it. The blue solid line is the 3 EMA based on a 15min TF. In theory it reminds me that the 15m is also holding my direction (as long as I pay attention to it). But, as with anything... Sometimes my eyes completely ignore them when desperation sets in. Call it blind panic.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok, try to come up with a way to simplify your charts. Less shaded areas and dotted lines. Stay with the essential. Since you use EMAs, I think thats great. But the problem is that the 3 EMA is too fast, and the 3 EMA in the 15 min TF is too slow, and you are in between them both with an 8 EMA. 15 min 3 EMA = 45 bars avg price of 1 min in 1 min TF. I suggest, you can rid of EMA 3 in 1 min TF, rid of 3 EMA in 15 min and work with an EMA that can pair with your 8 EMA, ie, 21 EMA or 20 SMA. Then, you can see how much they help you find an entry and navigate a trade.

author: Kam
content:
I think the key is to really switch the focus to the logic or 'why' behind the level. The reason we trade BnR and key levels is because we are looking for evidence of prior resistance turning to support and vice versa. The lower wick on the 9:56 candle shows that buyers are stepping in to defend 211 - a key level both intraday as well as last week's high. There were multiple confluences here. 3c close below such a key level on a $200 underlying price wouldn't be enough evidence to stop out for me personally. But if your data & backtested rules say to stop out, then definitely stop out!  

That being said - it's really easy to use hindsight bias on this one as the trade worked. If you were in the trade from <@722212337460838421> entry and holding as that lower wick forms, likely you're going to want to stop out at lows as it pushes below your stop. The confidence to place stops consistently and execute usually comes from really understanding & internalizing the logic behind the level and of course there's no substitute for a lot of reps where you practice doing the same thing repeatedly. I'd really suggest checking out Jack's video on accepting risk - it's a really good one and worth reviewing https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222375088318447637/1467221561898569911

author: Martin_AT
content:
One day later I looked at the trade again and I found out the mistake I made and why it confused me. Simply the entry was too late. I make this mistake from time to time and this usually leads to a bad RWR. So when something like yesterday happens my SL gets triggered and then price moves to TP.
So the logical "solution" for me is to really focus on entries very close to the level and if the trade is already gone not tho chase the trade anymore.
In the screenshot you can see that if you just move the Long-Tool a bit lower then the trade would have worked out. So simple in hindsight but sometimes very hard to understand during the "hectic" trading session. Therefore will adapt my "daily remainder" so that I can avoid this in the future.
Maybe you had the same issue and this helps you as well

author: Martin_AT
content:
for me the entry point was was also good BUT only the timing was off and this can be a huge downside. So here is the entry that would have been optimal for me. It's the exact same SL/TP only with a slightly earlier entry. During the same time QQQ was also moving up after creating a potential HL in the 1min.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/BURYd40B/

Earlier entry also means that the SL is lower. In this case the SL would have been just below the 5min range (so a reasonable SL compared to the first screenshot). No candle close below the SL and therefore this entry would have worked.
Such a "small" detail but in this case this led to a loosing day for me instead of a winning day. In options trading sometimes just a few seconds can make a huge difference. This is just another example for me that I need to be fast on my entries and avoid entries that are too late / too far away from the level. If however a late enry can not be avoided then my remainder is to simply use a wider stop which would have also worked in the nvidia set up. The learning/refining of ones playbook never stops xD

author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

author: Humantorch
content: If you do marker order, I've been there myself many times. It's a mental game as much as it is technical. Focus is a big part of it. Once you see price starts moving to your TP, you have to get ready with the finger on a trigger. Sometimes the move happens slowly and you have lots of time to close it. Other times there are pops in price and you have to be fast to close your position otherwise it MAY reverse completely and you don't get a chance to close it, wait for maybe another chance and it goes red and you end up losing the trade.

author: Humantorch
content: I think a big part of it is realizing your mistakes and weaknesses, and trying to improve them. Improving execution and mental focus game is one of the most important parts after you learn the technicals. And nobody is perfect enough to be on point every time, so we keep improving and refining and coming to the battlefield ready and focused.

author: Dice56
content: I’m now 2 weeks into this group and so far one of my issues is that when I go into a trade it takes me a moment to see the price of the contract and to fill it and I’m wondering if I’m better off just going with a market order for the sake of speed and getting into the trade faster and out faster too when it’s time to scale.

author: Rodo
content: Honestly at this stage, just use market orders. You’re still learning, so speed and execution matter more than getting the perfect fill. Right now the bigger issue is hesitation and missing entries—not a few cents on price.Once you get comfortable with that and everything starts to feel more natural, then you can build on it step by step—and eventually switch over to limit orders as you refine your execution. Also, it depends on your size. If you’re trading a small number of contracts, market orders won’t hurt you much. But as you start trading more contracts, getting filled efficiently matters a lot more—so that’s when limit orders become more important.

author: 305 Trader
content:
ok. Two things. One is from pure observation and keeping stats and the 2nd one is a recent development. 

-  when the adverse force prior your entry, is a dwindled and reduced candle, you can safely enter on your rising bar color which takes out that adverse coor bar. So then, to that extent you entered late. But, since the previous bars were evidently with long wicks it means, market indecision. So if you take an entry following those wicks, you have to off load heavy weight at the HOD or top of those wicks as an options trader. If futures, not as critical. 

- You have to have a thesis of the PM iin 1 HR tf if you are to enter in the firsr 30 mins of the market open, what was the structure formed? The price after the opening,  will revisit those PM levels and react strongly. Check on this in addition to the PD key levels.

author: 305 Trader

author: Jatin
content:
You have already answered your questions. The contracts on NVDA move a lot less if you compare to AMD contracts. Just an example, if you are trading 4 cons in AMD, the usual risk is around $60 per contract. Say you lost that trade and on 4 contracts it will be -$240 on that trade.

Then you trade 4 contracts on NVDA, with usual risk being $30 per contract. Say you won this one and took a full exit at 2RR. Now you won and profit comes to $240 for your 4 contracts.

See now you are break even even after you took 2RR winner. The issue is sizing. On the first trade you were risking $240 but on second one you were risking $120.
To match the risk in the trades, you would have traded 8 contracts on NVDA with risking same $240 on the trade, now if you won at 2RR, you would have profitted $480

Let me know if this makes sense

author: Jatin
content:
<@1455167515926728907> <@1492137844276334827> What I have been following is very simple to follow. Helpful for small accounts, this will lead to more consistency than home run trades. Try to follow this:
If you have 1-3 cons, take full exit at 2RR. If you have 4 or more, scale out minimum 75% of position on 2RR and leave rest for next targets. This will make it simple to follow, focuses on consistent base hits instead of rare mooshots we aim for sometimes with greed or whatever.

The number of cons depend on the risk amount on cons for the trade

 Fwiw, I only ever use market orders on my sells, unless I'm trading something super spready (MU, META etc)  

There’s always going to be a little imperfection with fills/slippage but consistency in your own execution process is what matters, more than trying to optimize every exit perfectly

author: Kam
content:
Yeah that’s one of the harder parts with options. The cons don't always move with the underlying especially on fast moves, wide spreads, or later in the week. I have rules to size down on Thu, Fri even tho cons look cheaper

Another idea is to think  in “risk bands”, e.g' if your intention is to risk $100, accept that your real risk is likely $100-$175 depending on the contract dynamics

It’s never perfectly precise with options unfortunately, part of the business. We definitely all lose more than intended on those kill candles when they happen though. But with the right tactics you can put up some safeguards

author: 305 Trader
content: The higher prob set up for an entry, is not just any type of B&R, but rather, the B&R and continuation of the top of a price structure formed (ceiling), or the inverse, the B&R and continuation  of the bottom of the structure formed (floor). When you wait for this to happen you will win most of the time. Thus, the best entry in your screenshot was later when the $400 level was broken up, retested and continuation. Do this, over and over, repeat.

author: Uche
content: I appreciate this feedback. I see what you’re saying with the B/R of the 400 being the higher probability entry. In hindsight I felt confident entering on the PDH bounce because of the divergence and relative strength of GOOG.

author: 305 Trader
content: it is not because it was $400 the level. It can be any price level,  just allow  the structure to formed first as it happened in your move. Read what I wrote several times. Then backtest this event in the TF you like. Log your results. It happens in both directions. This is the highest prob set up.

Hey everyone, wanted to share a transparent update. I’ve officially wiped the $1k I started with.

This wasn't a failure of the strategy or the course, it was 100% my execution. My journal shows a "death by a thousand cuts" from:

- FOMO & Chasing: Entering late and forcing setups.
- IBKR slips / accidental execution
- Revenge Trading: Trying to "claw back" small losses and emotionally reacting.
- Stop Loss Management: I honored my stops, but didn't adjust/edit them properly for the price action.

Fortunately I only traded one contract and kept my position size small at all times. 

I’m stepping back to reset and build back the right way while trying to refine my edge.

author: Kam
content:
Hey there, 

The fact that you can clearly identify the actual behaviors so specifically is probably more important long term than the $1k itself.

For me personally, the biggest reset after rough patches has always been reducing the pressure to “make it back” and just getting laser focused on execution - especially if I know execution was the issue (vs. market or normal variance). I usually size down until I see that I'm trading my process / setups consistently again. 

Regarding drills - I think this depends on each person to a certain extent, but something that helped reduce my trade count was really focusing on my filters for (dis)qualifying a setup. If you try and actively ask yourself 'why not' when considering a trade it can help with awareness & logic vs just getting locked in on the patterns we've been trained to recognize in the charts. 

Hope that helps - love the attitude 💪

author: Kam
content:
Looks like a solid technical setup to me. Overall I'd say the HTF's on AMZN are more neutral so if you can find any pockets of space to play level to level like you did yesterday can def find some decent trade ideas. 

Generally speaking you want to be playing on the same side as the indices when trading the mega-caps. If you knew the catalyst for the downside and had conviction it would help, but otherwise I'd say lower probability if playing against the index

author: nasman
content:
That "why not" filter makes perfect sense. I definitely get locked into just seeing the pattern and ignoring the messy context around it. I'm going to implement that exact defensive mindset to help disqualify the sloppy setups.

To build on that and really strengthen my ability to identify high probability trades, what does your personal in the moment checklist look like? Is there a specific sequence of things you check off before you finally decide to pull the trigger?

I’ve been trying to discipline myself to look for multiple confluences, like checking key levels (PDH/PDL or a 5ORB) along with VWAP, a 9 EMA crossover, and indices support (SPY/QQQ) to make sure the market is behind me. But sometimes it feels like a lot to process simultaneously when the market opens.

author: Kam
content:
Awesome, I'm glad that helped! I would honestly try to keep things as simple as possible - when you backtest or review your trades, try and really break down the logic of the trade. What makes your best trades 'good', and vice versa for your worst trades. 

For me my checklist boils down to a few categories: location, context, setup quality & trigger.

But to your point, that can be a lot to process in the heat of the decision moment, especially on a 1m chart. It takes time and a lot of practice, but I would definitely start with your data and try figure out where your strengths & weaknesses are. 

Confluences can definitely add to the probabilities, but I'd say even this depends on your specific system.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hey JP, I dont know if you are a woman or a man, i just wanted to tell you that I felt both laugh and compassion with your message. Laugh because you said “we throw the brain out of the window”. As for the rest, sounds like you have tried many things but the one thing still eludes you is the adhering to simple rules after observing what works. The only answer is: keep trying to figure it out for your self. But, with only 1x con. No more. Forget about the notion of getting rich or making a living out of this. Just sit down and trade with just 1x con, adjust your entries. The market has turned difficult for the day trader (not the swing trader with capital to hold). Corrections are very brief, ranges are long for some symbols. so go long for the most part. I also had a bad probably worst month of the year in May. Also, because I departed from what made me profitable in 2026 to try a new strategy which proved it is not working in this market with the Elliott waves I was trying. So, what we have to do is to adhere to a set of 3-4 rules and pick up our brains from the garbage can and force it to sit, “just to sit”and to wait for the rules to present themselves in the form of a set up and hit it. But also to act early as soon as they are spotted, this is the flow, to be able to cruise in low speed for a while but to switch gear quickly when the rules present themselves or viceversa. There it lies the fluidity of the same day trader.

author: 305 Trader
content: the character of the day trader is = acuity. This takes training, trial and error to develop it. So, the only remedy is to keep going. But safely (pretty small size), until you get it.

author: 305 Trader
content: I figured out in my own understanding something that I wanted to share, perhaps it sheds a light too: Why is a retest to a break out or to a breakdown so important? Well, since a price structure is broken when a ceiling becomes a floor (call), or a floor becomes a ceiling (put), how do we know if this event (break of structure) will hold? Answer: With the retest and continuation. The retest allows you to "test" that key level becomes a wall to the opposite force, and not a revolving door that would invalidates your thesis.

on Friday my overall bias was correct because my bias was to the downside. QQQ dropped after market open and was below PDL but I still hesitated to go short on AMD. https://www.tradingview.com/x/9jDs9Idd/
For me the learning is this (more like a "re-learning" because I should have known this): If Qs is dropping for about 30min without a significant pullback then the chance for a bigger pullback or a reversal is too high and therefore shorting stocks on the 1min TF is too high risk. Thus in future I would focus on shorting opportunities at the beginning of the session and avoid late short opportunities like AMD von Friday. (Based on all the trade reviews from Tony/Jack that I have watched I noticed that they never go short too late - like me in this case)

What are your thoughts on this? Do guys also avoid late entries or is there something else I missed? Thanks

author: 305 Trader
content: you are missing a key element, in your analysis. Its the HTF. In the 1H TF and 4H Tf identify strong levels of support and resistance (often are pivot points), and plan your trades on the reaction to those levels, as well as use them to identify your exits. That move most certainly in AMD hit an important support level at 486 in 1H Tf. Take a look and you may see this. Do not trade if you dont make a thesis based on laying out a plan on these two HTF.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok <@1490749230837399662> thanks for sharing. This is what I think it happened against you. - You did not allow a solid retest to the PDH to occur. This is a must at a very important key level such as PDH. If by waiting so the move takes off w/o you so be it. 2nd, Even if you waited for a solid retest to PDH as it happened few mins later in your chart, the PMH which was still not revisited by then, was quite close to your entry. So that dwindled further your shot at target of 1:2, for at best that entry was a 1:2 with zero error for the buyers. Wait up for larger space in your potential run, it will give you higher probs and also do not rush the retest, it has to be noticeable as per Hayden's explanations.

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1490749230837399662> Think of a retest as a "micro-accumulation", or liquidity-grabbing, so since this is what it is, the more identifiable that event is, the more significant the reaction can be of the force you need it or expect it to react shall be.

author: 305 Trader
content: This is one good reason why we need to allow a proper retest to happen.

author: Torr
content:
The way I've managed this is two loses, I scale down for the day to half risk on everything. If I lose three in a row, I'm done. I only risk .5 R ever, so I only need 1.5 trades to go right to be back to green the next day. If I have a string of losing days (two in a row), I scale down at the start of the next day. Two green days, and I scale up again. My reasoning for this is that I'm losing either because 1. I don't have the edge I think I do (perhaps because market conditions have shifted without me knowing) 2. My head isn't right 3. I'm just hitting a losing streak, which is probalistic certainty in trading. Either of these options mean it's better to just scale back or stop. 

But I also get it that we get hijacked sometimes.

author: Royal191
content:
Guys, it’s a learning process. I am in the same boat as some of you where the first modules are pretty slow. At first I thought about mentioning it too, but then I watched the recap videos and the live sessions ( even though I was present for the live sessions). These videos are sometimes 2 hours long and provide a ton of value. Seeing the thought process and reactions to certain things as they happen will not be seen in the modules. I am happy that Tony and Jdub are compromising and will now be releasing two modules per week; this shows us that we are in the right place and they are here to help us.

For the members that are saying it’s not fair for the month to month payers. Think about it this way, would it be fair for a month to month payer to access the modules they are looking for and drop off after one month because they learned everything they wanted? Now that wouldn’t be fair to the members that paid for the annual or the life time, would it? Tony said the reason for release timeframe is not a retention grabber since most of the members are either annual or lifetime, but think about this: Would anyone be willing to put countless hours and money into a program and sell it for $200? Because that’s essentially what happens when someone sign up for one month and access the modules they want then cancel, while others paid $1,000 to $2,000. The free content they provide on YouTube is worth more than $200 per month in my opinion, but that’s just me. Btw, I am one of the month to month payers myself, but that is because I am waiting to join the mastermind. We are all here to learn, trust the process. The other stuff will get worked out in time, it’s a new program...

author: Jdub
content: No one knows the exact amount that the  contracts will be worth at a certain price due to the greeks always moving. The reason I said 14 was my risk is becasue if you see on the options contract that was the break of strucutre level. If price closed below the key level the contracts would be worth around 14 ish. In this case it was an a+ setup. I usually risk around $2000 on A+ setups. Lets say I wanted to risk $3500 on a trade where the contracts are worth $1.75 then my stop would be at $1.40 on the contracts.

author: Jdub
content: Yes. When trading options espically right off of the open it can be hard to manage risk sometimes becuase price moves so quick. The easiest way is to look at the options chart real quick to give you an idea of how much your risking. If price is consolidating and you have more time you can calculate the risk amount with delta for a more precise risk amount. That's why when you risk a same amount per trade you understand lets say for example tsla I only need to give it 10% to know if I'm right or wrong. So if I usually risk 10% that means I will risk $750-$1000. That means my posistion size will be anywhere between $7500-10,000 position size depedning on the price of the contracts.

author: thespecialkid2.0
content: Got you! So now last question, let’s say you have 20k account how much are willing to risk on A+ setup, and how do you know how many contracts you are buying each trade. You run quick math or now since you’ve been trading for a while you know instantly your position size on each setup?

author: BackwoodsDisco
content:
I made a google spreadsheet that calculates it all, based on account size and how much I want to risk each trade. Once I used it for a while, I got the general idea and do quick math in my head
Say you have 20k and you want to risk 3% on each trade. That’s $600 risk per trade. If you set a 10% stop loss, then total contract value would be $6000. So, $6000 divided by the cost of each contract = how many contracts to buy.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: I'd like to share few things. It's been 2 months in this room. And i had my attention glued to mainly learning. Taken probably about 10-15 trades in 2 months😵‍💫  Could have really followed the call outs and made money  🤌  But i can't take something i don't understand. Here's a few things that really helped: Creating a proper playbook with screenshots and entry/exit criteria; Backtesting each setup (proper backtesting, i realized i did it wrong all those years); doing video trade reviews (same as Tony and Jake do on youtube. created a channel and posting it there) Helps so much! Also created a discord where i post my watchlist and my thoughts. Having my thoughts written out helps with discipline!!! Some days i think i didn't make any progress so i made a list of things i learned in 2 months and it's impressive. Next step is getting off the lives and doing my own stream so i can talk through my thoughts and watch my emotions. Will review posted lives for my mistakes and thought process. I really need to hear my own thoughts otherwise i'm distracted. Some people can do 3 things at once, but it's not me. My main message here i guess i can't do less than Tony or Jake and be profitable.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hi Gaby. It's never about account size, but about how much work you put in.
It's a different path for everyone, the emotions involved at the time of taking decisions on a Trade kick in differently.

author: phenomenia
content:
I agree to Neto Moreno: Only risk money you can afford to lose. Losing money is a bad feeling but when you loose money you need for other things its even worse.
I've started with 2200$ and after two weeks of consecutive green I've added further 2000 to scale up a bit to get more contracts to scale better. However, I've started the first weeks with only 2-5 contracts to see how the thing moves dollarwise and get a feeling for it.
I'm trading stocks for some years already so I know the charts and stuff but I'm quite new to options.
I also like the idea Tony mentioned in his last video: don't care about the dollar gains at the beginning. Focus on propper entries etc. Big profits come with that consistency then.

author: abhigs
content:
Hey <@717554586843611217> , I am not an experienced and profitable trader like you however I would like to point out some obvious observations overlooked on this NVDA trade today.
1.    On HTFs this was a gap fill and reject (daily gap) + Hourly 200EMA (rejected 3-4 times from there) sitting right there as a confluence
2.    QQQ opened very close to or in HTF supply and never broke above PDH’s 
3.    These 2 confluences above were strong enough to not enter this trade per my observation
4.    I found this trade impulsive rather than thoughtful based on HTFs
Hope you don’t take this negatively.

I wouldn’t say it was an impulsive trade since we trade momentum and on this pullback there is really no higher timeframe setups. Just a trade that didn’t end up playing out.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
I kinda count the risk per trade, to have equal risk per trade in all trades - 150$. Still I lost 294$ on Monday (150$ standard risk times 2 losses), 98$ yesterday (1 loss which was larger than expected, because half of the position I sold at breakeven), and today with pretty same risk amount I gained 24$ on 1 con AMD (sold at low of day) and 57$ on 3 cons AAPL (sold part at low of day and part a bit lower). 
How do you guys manage your risk to actually be profitable with, for instance, 2:1 win rate? I end up needing like 4:1 win rate with such stats, in order to be profitable.
I use market orders, maybe that's the reason - I buy a bit more expensive and sell a bit cheaper and with a small RR it ends up eating huge part of the profit

author: Brian
content: Don't go on tilt. Slow and steady wins the game. Put it behind you and take good trades to build it back up slowly. Every time I try to make a "get it back" trade it fails 100% of the time.

author: BFX
content: Don’t try to get it back asap because you will continue to fail, give a break and study your mistakes, then correct it with potential good trades, you will surely cover it in due time and learn how to cut your loses

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: That's a hard situation. If I were you, I would spend some time working on psychology, without trying to get the money back. Just strict risk management with small amounts of risk. And then trade with decent risk, because with a good RR and win rate you will get the money back in a short period of time, but only after you stop losing money doing things you weren't planning to do. First clear your mind, then back to trade.

content: This is the reality of trading. The past two weeks have been difficult since we've had a lot more ranging markets with v shapes. Currently the conditions have made it difficult and thats when we try and size down and take quality setups. author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: I have relevant problems, and I know some other people have these, because it's difficult to manage risk. If you enter with Tony/Jdub, it might be later than them because the video might lag behind. Thus stop loss is wider. Scaling out at HoD makes RR less. This might lead to losing the same amount on losing trades that you gain on winning trades (and no continuation on trailers). If you can't manage to risk each time the equal amount, which is hard because you don't always know the stop loss potential risk when you enter the trade, you might end up red.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey everyone,

We’re all here with the same goal: to learn and grow as traders. We’re like a family, supporting each other through the ups and downs. I know that red days can really throw our emotions off (it happens to everyone!), but it’s important to keep those feelings in check.

The Break and Retest strategy works, but it needs to be paired with solid risk management, the right mindset, good execution, no bias, and thorough preparation. Let’s keep growing together and focus on mastering these elements.

Remember, it’s all about your trades and your account. Let’s support one another and avoid any “I’m right, you’re wrong” mindsets.

Thanks for understanding and for being part of this awesome community!

author: Papa
content: Their risk tolerance is higher than yours and they can put on size appropriate to the market condition/sentiment.  Personally I was struggling for the first month, April, and then the market sentiment changed and I was doing well.  The past month hasn't been very good for me, so I'm re-evaluating what my mistakes are and there are two that stick out personally; FOMO and Market condition shift/no follow-through on tickers.  Being honest to yourself so you can work on correcting these things will help in the long run.  Base hits before home runs.

author: TheBat
content:
If it can help some people especially the one with smaller account here the things im trying to focus on . 

Today is my first red day in August, but overall this is a board of the habits i'm trying to focus on in order to judge or not if i have a good or bad day . As you can see even if today its my first red day my trading overall is not perfect at all. 
I started this the 26th juily 
- There is 4 times or I took a loss above my Maximum Loss per Trade (35USD)
- 3 time i went over my Daily max Loss of 70 USD
- 4 Times i took more than 3 trade a day
- 4 Time I took Trade without Displacement
- 3 time where i sold before my Target or my SL .. 

Im pretty sure we should all focus on stuff like this for turned profitable. Since i'm strictly focus on , even if i'm doing mistake my account is progressing and i starting to be more aware of my emotions and the mistakes that really slow me down. 
1 Day can fuck all the progress we make in a Week or in two Week and this is only cause we are not working enough on our habits.

B&R Strategy is not perfect, but she is working plenty of people show this everyday. Our main problem i think, its all the stuff that came beside (RM, psychology, knowing when to stop...) and there is only 1 person able to fix it even with the best mentor or signal in the world is ourself. 

Keep grinding, trust and respect the process, Tony said  it take time to change many years of bad habits and behavior

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**PRE-MARKET:** We laid out the game plan.

**WATCHLIST:** The same plan was shared there.

**LIVE VOICE**: We walked through the plan again and explained why we took calls.

Is everyone understanding why we take the trades we do and how we prepare and analyze the market?

Today, **there were multiple opportunities to enter calls on your own**. We also discussed how important it was for key levels to hold during the pullback for a move higher on different names.

If you’re just waiting for someone to tell you exactly when to buy and sell, **you’re missing out on the bigger picture**. I really hope more of you took the initiative and made those calls even after the initial callout.

We’re doing our best to not only provide  guidance and education to help you grow as traders but also solid callouts. Let’s make sure we’re all taking advantage of that.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Actually, when to enter and what kind of price action to seek on entry seems to me even harder than finding a working setup. Especially with small scalps to HoD, if you have to enter late, you better skip this trade. 

I rewatched some videos about the strategy recently, and I noticed that sometimes Jdub  enters the trade  upwards on the black candle of retest, without waiting for a green candle to form and show some displacement. Thus the risk is much smaller. That's a thing I want to look into, which entry conditions work better in which kind of trade and market.

Also, during the last month I've noticed (maybe it's just coincidental) that if the trade goes in the wrong direction or doesn't show impulse in the first couple of minutes, most of the times it will eventually exit on stop loss. I'm thinking of cutting the trades that don't seem to work earlier. Of course, there are trades where a wick touches the stop loss and the price goes up and reaches the PT, but doesn't seem to me that there are many of these.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

I never moved my SL to BE, I just scaled out 30-50% at HoD/LoD and scaled out the rest at psychological numbers and final target without any trailers. This led me to playing most of the time at RR 1:1, if not worse. That's why I decided to check the actual potential RR of the trades I took.

author: BullBearXInsights
content:
I’ve been part of several trading groups, and every time I joined one, I ended up blowing my account. When I traded on my own, I managed to make some profits. After joining this group, I blew my account again, and I realized it was because of me. I lacked focus and started relying too much on others' decisions instead of my own. I forgot that my purpose for joining the group was to learn from their trading mindset—how they think and trade. Believe me, Tony and Jack are the best compared to other groups out there.

One key lesson I learned was to do my homework, compare my analysis with theirs, and make decisions based on my own playbook. I started trading with smaller sizes, learning how they form their opinions. Interestingly, while I’m now closely aligned with their views, I still make slightly different decisions when it comes to entries and exits. I think these differences stem from their experience and account size. I feel more confident now, but as we all know, consistent trading success requires years of experience—at least three years of learning, and yes, blowing up your account (LOL).

The biggest lesson I’ve learned is the importance of cutting losses. There’s no reason to hold on to a losing trade. It’s like when you miss your stop at a train station—you should get off at the next one instead of waiting until the last stop. I hope everyone learns from their own experiences and finds success in the near future.

author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my st

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Slippage refers to the difference between the expected price of a trade and the actual price at which the trade is executed. This typically happens with market orders, which are executed immediately at the best available price. Since prices can change rapidly, especially in volatile markets or with large orders, the price you get may be slightly worse (or occasionally better) than the price you expected. 

For example, if you place a market order to buy a stock at $100, but by the time your order is executed the price has risen to $101, you experience slippage of $1. Slippage can happen in both buying and selling, and it’s usually more common during times of high volatility or low liquidity.

author: crunchybrewski
content: Most of the tickers we trade in this room have enough volume (contracts switching hands) that the bid-ask spread is pretty tight.  For a lot of my 0 day contracts, the bid ask spread is a few cents and there is enough volume to fill my order before the price moves too far in an unfavorable direction. For instance if I buy a contract for 1$ with the bid at .99 and the ask at 1.01. I can buy and immediately sell the contract for a loss of 2 dollars even without anything else changing because I bought at the ask and sold at the bid. tickers with less volume will likely have much wider spreads and so you can lose money even if the trade didnt move or moved in your direction. Hopefully that adds context to Neto's explanation.

author: asdf
content: I’ll contribute my two cents on this.  Brokers are allowed to give you the “best execution price”. So there’s no guarantee the price you get is the one you see on your screen.  In addition to the other examples given, huge price differences will occur during red folder news releases, earnings announcements and other high volume events.  From what I understand this discord does not trade during the announcements.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

author: Lane 🌿
content: I traded on my phone when I was a welder at a plant (super fast paced and strenuous) and had some success. I used WeBull because the mobile app is very very good and you can actually get in and out pretty quick. I bought one of those kickstand cases for my phone and I would put it in my welding cabinet where no bosses or anything could see. I kept 1 earbud in and had my sound on and notifications set on my charts for certain levels. Like for example the 5m range established so I'd go ahead and put an alert for above/below those levels by 1 cent. When they broke I'd get alerted, and when they'd come back to retest I'd get another alert! So I'd run over real quick and take my gloves off and get in the trade. Then I'd set an alert 10-15 cents above my stop so I'd get alerted and could stop out if needed. I'd have alerts 5 cents lower than each profit target to give myself time to get to my phone for the pop so I could scale out. I did this for about a year solid. It can be stressful, but once you kind of figure out your little way to do it and whatnot it can be done! Hope this helps man.

— Socrates
Highlights the importance of humility and acknowledging one's limitations in knowledge.

"The unexamined life is not worth living."
— Socrates
Encourages self-reflection and the pursuit of self-understanding.

"I think, therefore I am."
— René Descartes
Asserts that the act of thinking is proof of one's existence.

"Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it."
— George Santayana
Emphasizes the importance of learning from history to avoid repeating mistakes.

"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act but a habit."
— Attributed to Aristotle
Stresses that consistent actions shape our character and achievements.

"The greatest enemy of knowledge is not ignorance, it is the illusion of knowledge."
— Stephen Hawking
Warns against overestimating what we know, which can hinder learning and progress.

"Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted."
— William Bruce Cameron (often misattributed to Albert Einstein)
Reminds us that not all valuable things are measurable, and not all measurable things are valuable.

"Be the change that you wish to see in the world."
— Often attributed to Mahatma Gandhi
Encourages personal responsibility in initiating the changes one desires globally.
(Note: Gandhi expressed similar ideas, though not in this exact wording.)

"The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing."
— Often attributed to Edmund Burke
Highlights the importance of taking action against wrongdoing.

"Education is the most powerful weapon which you can use to change the world."
— Nelson Mandela
Emphasizes the transformative power of education in society.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey @everyone,

Just a quick heads-up on why trading options on FOMC days isn’t recommended. The market tends to be super volatile, with quick and unpredictable swings that can throw off even the best setups. Options can get especially tricky because premiums can spike or drop suddenly, making it hard to manage risk. It’s often better to wait until the market settles after the announcement, when you can trade with more clarity and less stress. 

Play it safe—there will always be better, more predictable days ahead!

<@&1219020123218645102>

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

content:
With the labor union strikes on the ports I feel like this isn’t quite priced into the markets…. Market reacting slowly on this. Unless deal reached quickly, market down. And good luck on the automation negotiations. Great reason to test the lows. We have our bearish catalyst.


The ongoing union strikes at U.S. ports will most disrupt public companies that rely heavily on international supply chains and the smooth flow of goods through these ports. Some of the most affected industries include:

1. **Retail Companies**:
   - **Walmart (WMT)** and **Amazon (AMZN)**: Both companies are highly dependent on imports for their inventory, particularly goods from Asia. The closure of major ports will lead to delays in restocking and higher transportation costs.
   - **Target (TGT)**: Similar to other large retailers, Target depends on a seamless supply chain to maintain inventory levels and meet customer demand.

2. **Automotive Industry**:
   - **Ford (F)** and **General Motors (GM)**: U.S. automakers rely on a mix of imported parts and components to manufacture vehicles. The Port of Baltimore, the largest importer of cars in the U.S., is one of the ports affected by the strike [oai_citation:3,2024 United States port strike - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_United_States_port_strike) [oai_citation:2,US dockworkers' strike paralyzes shipping, threatens economy – DW – 10/01/2024](https://www.dw.com/en/us-dockworkers-strike-paralyzes-shipping-threatens-economy/a-70372925).
   - **Tesla (TSLA)**: Tesla's global supply chain could face disruptions, especially regarding parts and vehicles shipped from overseas factories.

3. **Consumer Electronics**:
   - **Apple (AAPL)**: Apple could face significant disruptions, especially for its iPhones and other devices assembled abroad and shipped via U.S. ports.
   - **Sony (SONY)**: Electronics manufacturers like Sony depend on ports to import components and finished goods, which may see delays.

author: Polinka
content: Hi all, looking for an advice how to manage trading with a small account, 1000$.  I find it very challenging to set a correct stop loss. I didn't find any specific video on it in the accelerator. I saw Tony's video about growing a small account, but it wasn't a cash account, therefore not applicable for me in the US. I trade with one contract, and then find it challenging to hold for higher targets. So all together I am struggling with quick and correct set of the stop loss,  and at the same time I take profit on the first target level. Therefore, all together I loose. What is the minimum number of contracts that you can advise me to trade with at 1000$ account? How do you set loss in order not to get out of the trade on wickets? I understand that Jack doesn't set a hard stop loss. I find my self struggling with a quick trade cut, and then having too big losses. How do you manage it? Thanks in advance.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Maybe you should rethink your PT not stop loss. With 1000$ most probably you shouldn't trade with more than 1 con, with risk 30$ it's already 3% of the account, you don't wanna risk more. 
The stop loss depends on the entry, it might be below retest candle, below breakout candle, below OB if you enter off OB. Backtesting should tell you where to set stop loss for your exact setup.
But you need to take full profit on 2RR or 2,5RR or whatever RR is average in your backtesting and combines with the win rate to make the strategy profitable.

author: MVP Freestyle
content: Choose options that expire 3 months out and swing trade it for a one to a few days. Take a contract slightly out of the money and let that appreciate to target. When you see perfect setup within that position buy a weekly contract to add  leverage to position. Essentially you are using the 3 month contract as a placeholder to add additional leverage on a shorter weekly timeframe when conditions are a no brainer.

author: demchy19
content:
Hi Lauren,
I'm not a professional trader yet; I'm still learning, but I'll share my thoughts on your question. I don't think this follows the 84% rule. If I understand correctly, the 84% rule applies when you have a plan for entering a stock at a specific key level (e.g., the 5-minute opening candle print), and the stock tries to retest that level but fails and goes lower. The 84% rule comes into play when the stock returns to that key level and breaks through it. At this point, you just watch the price reaction at that level. Typically, with the 84% rule, the stock doesn't retest the key level—it just breaks through and moves in the right direction.
From the picture below, we can see that the stock has retested the same level twice. This can give you a stronger confirmation that the level is well established and could push the stock lower. There are likely many sellers above this level, preventing the stock from going higher, so there's a good chance it will drop. But based on your take profit and stop loss, you seem to be in a good position.

author: demchy19
content: Today, I was trading NVDA and followed the 84% rule. I planned to enter on the 1-minute OB, but it didn’t hold; instead, it went through. Once the stock reclaimed that level, it was a good entry point. I entered the trade, but I think I was a bit too quick and didn’t wait for the proper timing. Fortunately, the trade moved in the right direction.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

author: chunky_beast
content:
When it came to TSLA for example I looked at Call v Put volume in the few days before earnings. What caught my eye though was open interest on Tuesday & Wednesday. And it was very much with the calls. It was very much about a 70/30 split leaning towards a bullish. 

However I very much caution to say this kind of play is a “lottery ticket”. I based it on delivery info, calculated margins based on public info, and the option info. This is why I went a bit deeper OTM for 240C and 260C and risked about $2600.

I’m sure you know but to reiterate with earnings positions like this you need to be prepared to lose it all.

author: chunky_beast
content:
<@584423632802021376> I’ll keep this as simple as possible, but please what I’m about to say isn’t meant to be gospel. I don’t like leaving questions unanswered if I can help it. 

When you look at the upcoming earnings you obviously expect an uptick in options trading into that particular week volume wise but what will almost always increase is options holders/open interest (OI) into those options. Using AAPL for example we have a large disparity between Call holders vs Put holders for Nov 1 Weekly’s so with this we can estimate that, as of Friday, the market is currently expecting bullish move into earnings. Plus as you get from Earnings Whispers, on the wider options there was a large volume of Feb 21 265C bought on Friday indicating some bull expects a move towards the upside in the longer term worth putting up $12M. With all this you can infer the market a bullish outlook on AAPL. 

A different case is GOOGL where we can see, as of Friday, there isn’t really any high expectations for explosive short term moves based on next weeks options OI. 

There are other equities you can look at with extremely high bullish option sentiment like AMZN and AMD. 

However this is just a gauge, not a crystal ball. Do your own research and ensure that this correlates with your strategy and playbook. Also if you’re gonna trade shorter dated options into earnings this isn’t a swing where you may lose 60-70%. You can easily lose your equity so you have to be comfortable with a 100% risk to capital overnight.

author: Jdub
content:
Its not really about being upset because as a human you are going to have emotions. It's all about what you do with those emotions. Am I upset when I take a bigger loss than expected. Of course but its all about how you respond and what you do after the fact. 

Trading is all a game about probabilities. I had conviction coming into this morning on TSLA for a continuation higher and guess what it didn't work out. I wanted to only risk around $2000 on this trade and ended up losing over $4000. But what happened next, I didn't do anything irrational or went to do something dumb. What I did was wait for another opportunity to setup. 

We ended up getting in SPY/ES which was a solid setup and is still working out as I type this out. 

As for relying on trading as income I didn't do this for the longest time. When I started out and even when I was profitable I was still delivering packages on the side. I would next recommend someone to do this full time without some sort of income coming in. Having to "rely" on trading is going to make it very difficult no matter who you are. 

Hope that makes some more sense.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

author: Nines
content: Study ict core content, weekly profiles, but essentially any time i take a trade there has to be a key level. Study time and when price moves. If time is not right price wont go no matter what. This includes what day of the week and what time in the trading session. When looking for a key level the best ones are usually 1w,daily,4h and sometimes hourly but 1h key levels are for quick scalps, if u want to catch a good 100 point move mark out atleast 4h levels

author: Mike Y
content: I've been studying and reading my MM5 notes and a piece that I wrote from Jdub word for word (I write this so not only myself but for many to connect with) :  Higher Time Frame Thesis: If your so fixated on lower time frame 1 min chart and you always sell on pops and take profits, sure you might make $400-$500 on 1 min time frame, but when HTF trade comes you've always sold on its pop. So when you are in a HTF trade you can not hold for HTF move. You can't take higher profits because you always come in as a scalpers mentality and sell on pops.  If you focus on HTF you will be able to capture more of the move.  But since you have a 1" lower time frame trading mentality-you will never capture higher time frame PROFITS!     I hope this connects as I am totally guilty of this!  Granted if $400 or $500 is all you want, I get it, but we as traders are not here to be limited.  to be restricted, to be reckless of trade by fear.  We are here to Capture, to provide and broaden our discovery to trading.  We can do this and we got this!! Just give it time and be patient!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thank you so much for your kind words and for recognizing the progress I’ve made it truly means a lot. The turning point for me was realizing that trading is all about psychological thinking. I had to learn to trust my knowledge and analysis, stay focused, and, most importantly, put emotions aside. Scaling up became much easier once I disciplined myself to stick to my trading plan, even though the setups stayed the same.
I’ve failed many times in the past because I didn’t stick to my plan and let emotions take over. It wasn’t until I committed to following the plan and staying disciplined that things started to change. Your mention of the Plateau of Latent Potentialreally hit home it’s such a powerful concept. There were so many moments when I felt like all my hard work wasn’t paying off, but eventually, everything started to click. Breaking through that phase took persistence and mindset shifts, and it felt amazing.
And no, I wasn’t upset at all by your gesture it actually made me happy! It’s great to know someone else understands and celebrates these milestones with me. Thank you so much for your support and encouragement it truly means a lot. 

Trust the process:)

author: chino
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share today’s trade and ask for some advice. I sold at the first white line the moment i saw green, but look how much it dumped in my favor afterward, straight to my final take profit level.

This kind of mistake happens to me all the time. Fear always gets in the way, and I struggle to hold my trades. Today was especially challenging because the price was dancing near my stop loss, which didn’t help my confidence. On top of that, I assumed there wouldn’t be any strong continuation due to the holiday tomorrow and the market being closed.

What’s frustrating is that I still got rewarded despite making this mistake, which makes it harder to break the habit and learn to just HOLD!

Do you guys have any tips on overcoming this fear and holding trades longer? I feel like this is a major hurdle I need to get past.

author: Royal191
content: hi, It’s never a bad idea to lock in profits and end the day green especially on a day when price was choppy and right before a holiday. A tip to help you hold your trade longer is to take some off at your first PT so this way you can hold a small amount to your full TP and if price reverse you stop out of the remaining cons at break even.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

author: Angel (az)
content:
I can't speak for the pros, but here is my personal experience as a beginner - I'm able to hold the trade and not care because I am comfortable with the risk im exposed to at 2 cons total, I am comfortable with it dropping 50% in value overnight in the worst case. And 2 is easier to manage than 1 IMHO. I don't know how you're sizing but it needs to be what you personally are comfortable with mentally at this point in your journey. I am strict with scaling out at the first pop up which gives you realised gains and it's like a cheat code that makes it easier for you psychologically to hold the second one. The Tesla trade this week for example, my first con scale was 300+ realised, so now psychologically, i am okay with my second con going to -300. (I would not wait that long to stop out, but it lets you breathe and gives you a lot of room mentally). Ever since then, I literally try to not even look at the value of my second con or the price, I am just waiting for the HTF target to hit or structure to break. 

If the trade doesn't work: I believe Jack or Tony said you have your stop losses as usual in terms of trades not going your way, but you have to be more comfortable with a larger percentage drawdown. Stop loss is at where key HTF levels and structures are broken

author: Angel (az)
content: Np 🙂 stay as long as the market dictates since we can't predict when or if it'll actually happen. You just take it day by day and stay in your trailer con until 1) HTF take-profit target hits or 2) structure breaks, and stop loss hits. Case 3 I imagine is expiration day hits and neither 1 nor 2 happened, in which case I would probably reevaluate whether everything still looks solid, and close this week's and decide whether to buy next week's expiration. (Have to check with others on this).

author: Angel (az)
content: Oh and in case 1, if there are no more HTF levels then you start monitoring the chart real time and have to use more discretion/experience when to close out your last trailers. I would probably try to make myself a rule that's more mechanical since I still lack that experience. So probably something like - I must take profit after first bearish candle closes after ATH, or, after first close under an order block after ATH. That way I have a clear exit plan while I hopefully eventually gain that experience

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
It depends on your experience and ability to trade multiple things at the same time. There's no good or wrong way to do it.
If you're good with only 1 name in your watchlist and managing it's great, if you can watch 5 names and trade multiple positions at the same time it's also great.

author: demchy19
content:
Usually, red folder news affects the overall market rather than a specific stock. There’s always a possibility that the news will be good, causing the market to go higher, which would also take TSLA higher. On the other hand, if the news is bad, it could bring the entire market down, including TSLA. And sometimes, the news might not affect the market at all.

I’d say trading based on news is a bit like gambling—it’s always a 50/50 chance whether it goes in your direction or against it. Hopefully, that makes sense! 🙂

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Great question! Yes, I prepare a thesis every night based on higher timeframe trends (daily/4H) and intraday setups (15-min/5-min). I identify key levels, plan potential scenarios for both bullish and bearish moves, and focus on reacting to price action rather than predicting.

For your challenge, here are some tips:

Focus on the Bigger Picture: Higher timeframe trends often guide overall bias. Intraday bearish moves might just be pullbacks.
Look for Reversal Confirmation: Don’t assume quick reversals. Wait for signs like volume shifts, candlestick patterns, or break-and-retest setups.
Separate Contexts: Use the higher timeframe for bias and intraday charts for precise entries. Adjust your stop-loss and targets accordingly.
Review Your Trades: Journaling can help spot patterns and refine your ability to switch between timeframes.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Volume Analysis:

Check if the volume is increasing or decreasing. Rising volume often confirms strong momentum (Option A), while declining volume may indicate consolidation (Option B).
Key Levels:

Observe support and resistance levels (e.g., ORH, YDL). A bounce or rejection at these levels can suggest the next direction.
Relative Strength Index (RSI):

Use RSI to gauge if the stock is overbought or oversold. Overbought signals may lead to consolidation or reversal (Option B).
Candlestick Patterns:

Watch for reversal patterns like dojis or engulfing candles near key levels for a potential consolidation or reversal.
Trend Continuation Signals:

Look for lower highs and lower lows in a downtrend for continuation (Option A).
Market Context:

Consider broader market conditions and sector performance for confirmation of trend continuation or pause.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Practical Use of Indicators
VWAP (Volume Weighted Average Price):
Tracks the average price based on volume and price action.

General guideline:
Buy calls above VWAP.
Buy puts below VWAP.

Helps determine whether the price is overextended or needs a pullback.
EMA9 and EMA21 (Exponential Moving Averages):

EMA9 is for short-term trends, while EMA21 represents medium-term trends.
Commonly used for pullback entries in trending markets, especially on the 5-minute chart.
Pullbacks to these levels often coincide with key support, resistance, or liquidity levels.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Great second question! 
We typically align our trades with the macro narrative. For example, if the daily/1-hour/15-minute charts are all showing bullish signals, we're looking to enter on a smaller timeframe with a bullish bias. **That's what we consider an A+ setup - when all these timeframes align.** So, we usually aim for the high of the opening range when we're bullish and the low of the opening range when we're bearish. 

However, there can be scenarios where you might see a rejection at the Opening Range High or a bounce off the Opening Range Low, but these would generally need additional confirmation from the broader market context to be considered viable trades. 
Also, we always keep an eye on all relevant key levels like old highs and lows, pre-market levels, and previous day levels, looking for a retest on those levels and waiting for the retest and the eventual reaction. 

Keep an eye on the macro trends to guide your decisions! The trend is your friend!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: Order blocks and liquidity zones look different on 1-minute and 5-minute charts because they show price movements over different time periods. The 1-minute chart shows more small, short-term activity (noise), while the 5-minute chart combines those movements into bigger, clearer zones where major buyers or sellers are active. Use the 1-minute for quick trades and the 5-minute for spotting bigger trends and stronger levels

author: DWC2016
content:
Yea makes sense 100%. But risk and position size are not the same thing and often confused. 

Like Tony will have a position size of $30k but only risk $4k if the trade goes bad which would be %4 risk if the account if it’s a $100k account but if the trade works that how you get those $50k+ days while only risk a small amount. 

Or Ex : You can put on a trade that’s $1000 in size but only actually risk $100-$200 as the stop loss. I think a lot of people get confused between the position size and the stop loss which is the actual risk in the trade. Hope that makes sense. I was confused by this when starting as well 🙏

author: DWC2016
content:
Yea no problem I think this is a common missunderstanding. I traded crypto for about 6 years so about 80% of the concepts transfer over. 

But yea its a balancing act of the position sizing so you can make money when things works and the actual risk (stop loss) your willing to loss. The stop is the actual risk not the sizing unless you dont follow your stop loss rules then you will doom yourself 😅.

But In futures its a bit more straight forward to set a 20% SL but in options you have to do a bit of math. 

but you have to look at where your stop is and relative to the price of the contract cost if the underlying assest moves xyz percentage then the contract to move roughly the same. These is a video on youtube that explain these things. search " how to set a stop loss based on the underlying asset price" on youtube. 

Also if you type on youtube "how to set a stop loss with IBKR" you should see a few videos that show you. Its fairly simple.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
If you're a new trader or still working towards consistent daily trades, I strongly recommend starting with just 1-2 contracts to protect your capital. Focus on developing your own daily trading edge and strategy, as this is essential for long-term success.

Copying someone else’s trades might provide short-term results, but it’s not a sustainable approach. Instead, take the time to build your own foundation and confidence in your trading.

Additionally, PLEASE take the time to review the WHOP Accelerator Program and Tony/Jack's videos. They provide invaluable insights and teach you the essential steps needed to succeed in trading.

Make sure to identify the strategy that fits you best and stick with it. Remember, not everyone uses the same strategy, and trying to follow someone else’s approach may lead to confusion. Stay focused and committed to your personal trading journey!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Validate Trendline: Ensure it connects two or more significant swing lows/highs without cutting through candles. Retests confirm validity.
Trendline Break: Redraw if price closes decisively beyond the trendline with strong momentum or volume.
New Structures: Adjust for new formations, like flags or consolidations, as they develop.
Confluence Levels: Prioritize trendlines that align with key support, resistance, or Fibonacci levels.
Timeframe Matters: Short-term trendlines (1-min, 5-min) may require frequent adjustments due to volatility.
Price Behavior: Watch for tight consolidation or "riding" along the trendline as a signal for potential breakout.
Stay Flexible: Update trendlines dynamically based on real-time market changes and dominant structures.

author: Nines
content: Np, another tip is you go long until a key level on the HTF is been met. Because you have to think of it like this, price is not going to magically spawn to they key level. It has to get there somehow and there are alot of A+ setups that form to get you to that key level

author: Nines
content: This is essentially how you counter trend. Say your in a bearish market and you want to take a short but the next bearish key level is a bit far away and you dont wanna short at the current price because its over sold. You wait for order flow to switch from bearish to bullish on the 1h and 4h and then you take a long until that key level is met. Its crucial that you let a bottom form first and not just try and catch the bottom. Never ever try and predict the bottom u let it form and then play the counter trend

author: Nines
content: Daily and weekly is still bearish but 1h is now bullish and you play the long until the key level is met

author: Nines
content: I trade futures and forex mostly. In that market you only draw key levels on monthly, weekly, daily. Anything below a daily i dont bother drawing waste of time

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Headlines on bloomberg article regarding DeepSeek and AI market. 

“DeepSeek, a Chinese AI startup that’s just over a year old, has stirred awe and consternation in Silicon Valley after demonstrating breakthrough artificial intelligence models that offer comparable performance to the world’s best chatbots at seemingly a fraction of the cost. 

DeepSeek’s emergence may offer a counterpoint to the widespread belief that the future of AI will require ever-increasing amounts of power and energy to develop. 

The app distinguishes itself from other chatbots like OpenAI’s ChatGPT by articulating its reasoning before delivering a response to a prompt. The company claims its R1 release offers performance on par with OpenAI’s latest and has granted license for individuals interested in developing chatbots using the technology to build on it. 

Though not fully detailed by the company, the cost of training and developing DeepSeek’s models appears to be only a fraction of what’s required for OpenAI or Meta Platforms Inc.’s best products. The much better efficiency of the model puts into question the need for vast expenditures of capital to acquire the latest and most powerful AI accelerators from the likes of Nvidia Corp. That also amplifies attention on US export curbs of such advanced semiconductors to China — which were intended to prevent a breakthrough of the sort that DeepSeek appears to represent.”

author: StevenP
content: the 119 is 1 otm since stock price is 118. 1 otm strikes are a good balance for day traders to take. usually they have a good delta and the cost is decent. so it makes for good profit compared to cost. if you look at 2 otm sometimes the volume and open interest paired with your thesis on thinking the stock will go to $120 from 118 can be more profitable with a strong move towards the strike price. its really all about your risk and thesis on where the stock will go

author: StevenP
content: When I go further otm options I’m doing a swing or a leap. If I’m 2 or 3 otm I’m looking at open interest and volume with what the ask price is and have a target and good thesis setup for that strike. This is my personal preference. Others may have a different view

author: PewSpatialPew
content:
in general i think of it this way. Each con ATM or 1 strike away, the delta is 0.5 give or take.
That means for each $1 move in the stock, each contract adds about 50$ in value if it goes in the direction you expect.

if you have a tight stop loss, you can size up more cons, and vice versa, a bigger stop loss, you can downsize more cons.

author: PewSpatialPew
content: if you're gonna do OTM, you really need to expect the move to hit your target so the delta works out. else the loss just kinda compounds

author: PewSpatialPew
content: a smaller delta, means the value of the con moves less per move. So if a ticker isn't going towards your strike, your theta decay is gonna kill you. Delta only expands one you're ATM or ITM and then you can get the delta explosion as JDUB mentions about the contracts value.
