author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**__RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY__**

Listen up, because this is crucial. Take a screenshot of this and pin it somewhere visible.

**If you're in the green for the week**, only gamble with a tiny slice of your gains. Say you're up $1,000, then play with just $200-$300. Even if you blow it, you'll still walk away with a profit for the week.

If you're green but your profits are too slim to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you're in the red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

And even if you're sitting pretty with some profits to burn, consider taking Friday off. An extra day to relax isn't something to sneeze at.

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. __**

Follow these rules, and you'll never end the week wishing you hadn't played.

Please confirm you've read this with a 🤑

author: Moni Leine
content:
Hey Traders,
@everyone

Another fantastic Weekly Recap in the books! Here’s what we covered:

✅ Reviewed this week’s trades
✅ Reviewed mistakes made and how to avoid them in the future
✅ Key lessons to improve our strategies:

- Trade setups: 1' & 5' OR BNR (Opening Range Break & Retest)
- PDH/PDL BNR: Previous Day High/Low Break & Retest
- Key levels: Based on High Timeframe Thesis
- 84% Rule Strategy: a second high-probability entry at the previous BNR point after being stopped out on an initial trade.
- Choosing strike prices for contracts
- Managing stop losses:
🔹 1-BBB (Bar-by-Bar)
🔹 OB (Order Blocks)
🔹 Trailing stops
🔹 Break-even stops
🔹 Hard stops
With the market shifting and volatility on the rise, scalping is becoming the go-to strategy over swing trading. But don’t worry—we’re adapting together!

Jack answered all your questions—huge thanks to him! 🙌

These sessions are packed with valuable insights, creating a friendly and engaging space where our Coach is always ready to share knowledge and help us level up. Let’s keep taking advantage of these opportunities to improve together!

🚀 See you at the next session!

Cheers,
Moni

YES, all sessions are always recorded.
YES, it takes a few hours to upload, look for them a few hours later.
YES, if you have questions, you can always send them here to be answered (try to @ the Coaches or the Mods).

author: Moni Leine
content:
Hey Traders,
@everyone
Another solid session in the books with our coach JDub! Here’s what we covered today:

🔥 Weekly Trades Recap
Reviewed key setups and how they were executed.

📌 Entry Models
Conservative
Most Conservative
Aggressive

⏳ Timeframes & Execution
Thesis is built on 15’ charts to identify key levels.
Entries are executed on the 1’ chart for precision.

📍 Drawing Levels
Levels are drawn on the 15’ timeframe to track price action.
Targets are adjusted based on liquidity levels.

📰 JDub’s Pre-Market Prep
1️⃣ News Analysis – Understanding market-moving events.
2️⃣ HTF Market Context – Evaluating trends on higher timeframes.
3️⃣ A+ Setups Identification – Looking for high-probability setups based on:
PDH BNR (Previous Day High Break and Retest)
PDL BNR (Previous Day Low Break and Retest)
OR BNR (Opening Range Break and Retest)

📈 Risk & Position Management
Jack’s risk plan – How he manages trades on 1’ and 5’ timeframes.
Sizing Decisions – Adjusting position sizes for different trade setups.
"Look Left" Strategy – Define targets based on liquidity levels.

📊 How to Scale Out of a Trade
Scaling out in stages helps lock in profits while letting winners run. Here’s the approach (with an example, but you can set your own scaling plan):
1️⃣ First Exit – Secure Partial Profits (25%)
Sell 25% at the first key level to lock in profits.
2️⃣ Second Exit – Reduce Risk (25%)
Sell another 25% as price moves further in your favor.
3️⃣ Third Exit – Maximize Gains (25%)
Sell 25% when price hits a major breakout level or extension target.
4️⃣ Final Exit – Let the Runner Go (25%)
Let the rest run using the bar by bar method (trail stop with each new bar on the 1’ timeframe).

Thanks for joining! These sessions are packed with actionable insights—keep showing up, refining your skills, and taking advantage of these learning opportunities.

📅 See you next time! 🚀

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
To gauge QQQ's relative strength/weakness, compare it to:

📌 SPY (S&P 500) → If QQQ outperforms, tech is strong. If it lags, tech is weak.
📌 IWM (Russell 2000) → Strong QQQ vs. IWM = large caps favored. Weak QQQ = shift to small caps.
📌 QQQ/SPY Ratio → Rising = QQQ stronger, Falling = weaker.
📌 Bond Yields → High yields = QQQ weak, Low yields = QQQ strong.
📌 Volume & Breadth → Strong volume = real strength, Weak volume = possible fake move.

Hope that helps!

author: NickA
content: joined only 1 week ago... Copying them straight up has given me winners and losers.... but you have to handle your own risk/sizing/etc.  Try to make it about the process and evolving away from copying T and J to following along to learn the decision making and flow... easier said than done...  improve scanning technique on multiple symbols and id'ing the key levels with them....  study their pre-market strategy and real-time thought processes and adjustments... it's game changing for me...  seeing them in action helps me modulate my trading frequency and build a better eye for hitting better trades.   Still trying come up with my own actionable trades with better entry/exit plans every day and calming down in general.  Using their actions and comments as a "confluence" but not the only reason to jump in a trade is the pattern I am trying to break.... I am gaining more comfort with the idea of doing it solo. Still so much growth required (and accelerator training) but it's starting to slow down and feel more settled thanks to both T, J, and the community.

I need to 100% commit to my stop loss.

author: Angel (az)
content: What is your win rate on backtesting and what is your TP target when backtesting, are you just looking for 2:1? I'll be honest, when I first backtested, I had 40% win rate which was fine but could never quite get to 2:1 in my testing. So i kinda trusted the win rate and then learned the scaling correctly in real time because there'a a lot more contextual stuff, market conditions, higher time frames. If your win rate is okay and your ratio is not quite 2:1, then a lot of it might have to do with how you are taking profit as well.

author: DarthTrader
content: I base it off the underlying technical level. I do the math on how much I want to risk per contract and take it accordingly. I actually scale into my position so my initial position isn't huge and my losses are smaller as most trades fail in the first few min if they don't go right away. Once the make a new high or low, I actually add to my position (full) then adjust SL as needed. By this time usually we are breaking the highs/low and retesting. If that fails then break even it is. If not we are good.

author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

author: Nines
content:
some food for thought for you guys; 

scaling out or taking partials are for those who dont trade the bigger picture. reason u never see me scaling out a contract and holding to my tp because it skews my RR significantly. instead of making 6R ill make only 2.5R or lower. then you wonder why your losses are as big as your winners or even bigger. 

with option its tricker because you almost have to scale out because greeks will kill you if you dont, but if you trade futures or forex i recommend you hold to your tp because the days you lose which you will, it wont hurt because your constantly catching 5R trades on the regular. 

im not saying this to bash on anyone who scales out if that works for you and you have success doing that then keep doing it but for me in my experiene i used to see my losers were as big as my winners even tho i was catching 6R trades but when i would scale out at hod or a random level i had marked it skewed with my RR

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
When Walmart WMT releases its earnings, it often includes commentary from executives about consumer behavior—such as shifts in discretionary spending, preferences for value-driven purchases, or e-commerce growth—which can signal broader economic trends. 
Given Walmart's size and competition with companies like Amazon and Target, these insights can influence investor perceptions of those retailers as well, often leading to movements in their stock prices. 
For example, if Walmart reports strong sales and optimistic guidance, it might boost confidence in the retail sector, lifting stocks like Amazon or Target. Conversely, a weak report or cautious outlook could raise concerns about consumer spending, pressuring those stocks.
It's not a separate report, it's inside the ER.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@402084107951144973>  - 

🔍 1-Min Chart (Left Side)
More sensitive / reactive to short-term price movements.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline on the 1-min chart is drawn tighter and shows earlier signs of reversal.

This chart captured a break of the downtrend earlier, showing bullish momentum forming right after Scale #3.

Best for quick scalps or precise entry/exit signals if you're managing a trade closely.

Shows micro price action and short-term exhaustion or trend shift faster.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for day traders wanting to lock in profits quickly or protect positions once momentum shifts.


 5-Min Chart (Right Side)
Less sensitive and shows more confirmed structure.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline here is wider and slower, but gives a stronger trend confirmation.

The trendline broke after Scale #3, confirming a larger shift in trend on a higher timeframe.

Gives more trustworthy signals when looking for bigger moves or validating a trend reversal.

Smoother and filters out noise compared to the 1-min.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for swing entries, trailing stops, or holding positions longer with more confidence.

author: Kam
content:
hey there, while the key levels to take a trade may be different - the qualifying criteria for a break & retest at any level is the same (e.g. clear displacement; strong/weak price action at level, etc). 

using the 1m opening range is usually a more aggressive entry model, so if using the 1m OR we ideally like to look for confluences at that level (orderblocks or an opening play like a gap & go scenario, pre-market level, news, etc). 

Because of the big gap-down today and aggressive continued selling at the open, using a more aggressive entry model on shorts was necessary to try and capture continuation since in most gap-down scenarios we like to look for pops into higher timeframe levels before looking for setups - this generally leads to a higher probability & longer duration trades on the short side.

the best way to get deeper on this to to backtest and log some data to figure out which levels work best for you and on which tickers

also check out Tony's trade review on NVDA today; he goes over why the 1m opening range setup nicely after the gap-down with orderblock & PML confluence

<#1219022089252503632> 

 hope that helps!

author: Kam
content:
hey <@1323448182851637345>, I'm assuming your trade was a BnR off the 5m opening range. a few points to consider: 

1) on higher timeframes we opened up right in the middle of yesterday's range on the ES, so in order to go long or short at an intraday level like the 5m OR, you ideally need stronger evidence of momentum in either direction. Try zooming out to the 15m or 1hr timeframe to see the bigger picture 

2) the amount of displacement is key for the BnR system. In this case there wasn't a strong push or super strong candle close above the level you're using for the retest. Note the upper wicks (a tell for sellers) on the push above the 5m range on the 1m chart

3) if your entry was on the 9:57 candle, it's likely you didn't wait until the candle closed for confirmation that your level was holding. Remember you want to see strong price action at your level in order to enter. 

Will post a couple of links to videos Jack has made recently that may help with building higher timeframe narratives for your trade ideas as well as good entry confirmation signals

author: DWalt Trades
content: You have to also take into consideration that with a smaller account size, you are not going to be open to taking a risk size that Tony may be open to taking. So find what setups within the systems criteria work best for YOU, your skill and experience level, your management, your account, and your psychological acceptance to risk. Plan your trade and trade your plan. Understand and accept your risk before you take it. If you scroll through and take a look at some members trades you will see that we all trade the system in our own way. Backtest it and do the same! All of us take losers as well. author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Part of the risk with holding overnight and over weekend. A war could breakout, terrorist attack, natural disaster, fraud report, etc. 

Only trade with what you’re willing to lose because overnight is a crapshoot. 

Very unfortunate that Moody released the downgrade. I would even guess it was strategically timed as the market has rebounded from earlier lows. We just have to accept it as part of the game. 

Once you have a proven system to trade and confidence from back and forward testing, it is easier to accept the market for what it is, uncertain. 

Your edge in uncertainty is your back and forward tested system with proven probable edge and sound risk management. 

You have to know if we go to war overnight you’re okay with losing whatever is in the market overnight. 

I have lost quite a bit from following alerts and it has made me a better trader.

author: Jose96
content: Maybe you’re not sizing up and down correctly based on your stoploss. Usually your SL should be somewhere in the chart that invalidates your trade thesis. Based on your entry, you will sometimes have 20-30cents stoploss, and sometimes 1-1.5dollars. If you want to keep your losses the same, you shouldn’t size the same when your SL is 30cents vs when it is 1 dollar. For example if you are okay losing around 100$ per trade and aiming for at least 200$profit, for a 20cent SL, you could buy 10 contracts (assuming 50dollars per 1dollar move of the underlying, 0.2x50x10cons=100usd), but if your SL is 1dollar you could buy 2contracts to play it safe(assuming the same 50dollar per 1dollar move of the underlying, 1x50x2=100). Both cases, if your stoploss hits you’ll lose around 100$(maybe a little more bcoz how options behave). At least that’s the way i size up to keep my losses small.

author: GavinX
content:
My paper trading on IBKR

today spent all day just learning the interface and took some options trade, got familiar with how to get in and get out of options contract

I still not sure how to scale up and scale down the options contract, do i have to buy options contract out of the money call options all the time or i can always go back and  take in the money call during consolidation of underlying asset

author: Jose96
content: What i explained was based on the stock itself, but trading the option contracts. Say a stock is at 200 and based on market structure, the stoploss would be a break below 199.8. If you buy calls when the stock is at 200, your stoploss would be a 20cent move. If you buy 10calls, and you assume every will decrease 50$ per 1dollar move of the stock, a 20cent move will equal approx 100usd loss (0.2$ x 50$ x 10cons = 100). It wont be exact becoz of how contracts work, but should be a decent rule of thumb if you buy 1otm contract(40-50delta). If the SL is at 199, thats a 1dollar move, so if you want to only lose around 100usd if you lose the trade, then you would buy 2contracts (1$ x 50$ x 2cons = 100). If you buy the same 10cons for a 1dollar SL, you could lose around 500usd (1$ x 50$ x 10cons = 500). This is just something i use to sort of know how many contracts i’m okay buying for a specific trade opportunity, hope it helps a little

author: Elvis
content: I spent the day to figure out the options buying power so I hope this helps others as well, the contract price does not mater as buying power it’s the stock price that needs to be used to size. Let’s say a contract delta is .5. Tesla price is 345 and nvidia price is 135. The options will move 50 cents on 1 dollar move in the stock price. So if you have a 1% move on nvidia this would be 1.35 dollars so the contract will move about 67 cents and same 1% move on Tesla will be 3.45 dollars so the contract will move 1.72 dollars. So to get same size and risk consistently buying power needs to be measured by stock price. So if your consistent buying power is $1000 that you always need to buy 1000 worth of stock price not contract price to have consistent buying power. Hope others find it helpful, I spent some time doing options calculus haha. But again this depends on delta and that you are always taking the same contract for example always one out the money.

author: GoodVibez
content: At what Price point did you enter your shorts? This is something that I've kept in mind as I've experience a few big hit and have seen some comrades take some massive hits. This one thing... the market is predisposed to go higher. It's nature. We plant seeds so they can grow. Big Institutions want more, not less. So moves down aren't beneficial in the broader picture. I only enter puts in the short-term sense. IF it hits my stop loss or doesn't move towards my target with immediate conviction... I'm out that trade. I also don't leverage as much when entering shorts. Holding shorts for swings is a no-go in my rule book. I wait for big retracements or breakout levels and buy UP for time out. The likelihood your cons recover with longs/calls is higher. So the general consensus...CUT YOUR LOSSES, before the losses become too large for you to recover from. Can't recover if you don't have any funds to recover with. And the longer you wait... the more theta will eat your liquidity.

author: ma
content:
The key to trading options lies in momentum and volatility volume needs to come in.
If you're trading zero-day options and your stock doesn't move in your favor or just consolidates around  the first 10 to 15 minutes, you're likely to lose premium. Even if the move eventually goes your way, unless it's a big and immediate shift, recovering that lost premium is tough.
The best approach Cut your losses quickly.
Options are tricky. That's why traders like Tony prefer trading during the early market hours your trade usually plays out within 5 minutes. You either win or lose, but you avoid time decay, which is an extra loss that many beginners overlook.

Only consider swing trading if you're truly skilled at reading charts and fully understand market conditions. In today’s market, a single news event can invalidate all your technical levels news plays a major role.

author: Sean
content:
I delt with this alot recently. Slowly starting to over come it, but there are a couple things that come to mind. 

One of the bigger things that helped me was changing my goals. I used to focus on profits and losses. Money. Greed and fomo were getting to me. Especially when seeing others prosper. 

I decided to switch to more process focused goals (i.e. Did i follow my plan, respect my stop, respect my take profits...etc).  Doing so , I think, in time the profits will flow. 

Also realizing that is more about probability.  If you take the same trade/setup every day over a long period of time, you'll win out in the end if you have a proven edge in the market. Backtest & journal. Reflect. Have set max loss per trade and max loss per day, and max amount of trades per day. Implement what helps regulate those emotions in other aspects of your life when you feel them arise. For me, shutting things down and walking away and exercising helps settle things down. 

Hope this helps. 

Don't give up. This is a long drawn out battle against ourselves. Master the mind, master the markets.

author: Bhargav
content:
From $500 to $7,876 – My Journey of Patience, Discipline, and Belief

Hey everyone,

I wanted to take a moment to share something close to my heart – not to boast, but to hopefully inspire anyone who's feeling stuck or uncertain in their trading journey.

I joined this trading community about a year and a half ago. Back then, I was just eager to learn, hungry for growth, and like many of you, figuring things out one step at a time. It wasn’t always smooth. There were days filled with doubt, losses that made me question everything, and moments when I wondered if this journey was really for me.

But I kept showing up.

I studied, practiced, journaled my trades, learned from my mistakes, and leaned on this community for support and accountability. Every win and every loss taught me something valuable – not just about trading, but about myself.

In June, I decided to take on a personal challenge: turn $500 into $5,000. Not only did I hit that milestone, but I ended the challenge with a $7,876 account.

Yes, the numbers are exciting – but what I’m truly proud of is the mindset it took to get there: patience, consistency, emotional control, and unwavering belief.

This journey has shown me that it’s not about overnight success – it’s about persistence, process, and trusting that your time will come if you don't give up.

To anyone in this community who’s still grinding, still learning, still waiting for that breakthrough: keep going. You’re closer than you think.

Much love and gratitude to all of you who've been part of this path. The journey is far from over — let’s keep growing, together. Big Thanks to <@717554586843611217> , <@801169196015943731> and all the Mentors here.

– Bhargav

author: Brute7
content:
This was drawn manually before I created the script, and I hope it helps illustrate my experience.

Without R3 marked, many intraday traders might continue holding their positions, expecting further upside. Due to FOMO, they may end up selling only after the price starts dropping. Since I have R1, R2, and R3 plotted, I would sell a few contracts at R1 and the remaining at R2 and R3.

Again, at R1 we saw strong price action, which drove the move all the way to R3 and beyond. This allowed me to re-enter the trade at R1, sell a few contracts at R2, and the rest at R3.

author: Brute7
content:
I was able to complete script for ( Intraday Session Levels (Pre-Mkt, 5min, 15min)) and will only publish after testing it tomorrow. 

Quick glance of this indicator. 

Intraday Session Levels: Pre-Mkt, 5m, 15m (Auto-Reset)
Automatically plot the most important live intraday levels for each trading day—no manual work required!

**What this script does:**
**1. Pre-Market High & Low:**
Tracks and draws the live high and low from 4:00 AM to 9:30 AM ET. These are key reference points as regular trading opens.

**2. First 5-Minute Candle High & Low:**
After 9:35 AM ET, automatically draws horizontal rays for the high and low of the opening 5-minute range (9:30–9:35 AM ET).

**3. First 15-Minute Candle High & Low:**
After 9:45 AM ET, draws rays for the high and low of the initial 15 minutes (9:30–9:45 AM ET), used by many for breakout/reversal setups.

**4. Automatic Cleanup:**
All plotted levels are cleared after 4:00 PM ET, so your chart is always clean for the next session.

**5. Fully Automatic, Live Updates:**
The script updates in real time, resets for each new day, and handles all session times in U.S. Eastern Time.

Anyhow 

I have published with two new releases. 

Release Notes
**v2.0 Update – Gap Detection Added**

- Added automatic gap up/gap down detection and labeling at the 9:30 AM regular session open.
- Gap is visualized as a color-coded ray with the gap value, making overnight market moves instantly visible.
- Enhanced reliability for session edge cases (Mondays, holidays).
- Improved code for even cleaner chart visuals.

v3.0 — All labels centered!

All level and gap labels now appear at the center of each horizontal ray for the cleanest look.

No more clutter at the left/right chart edges.

Gap label is also centered.

Cleaned up variable scope warnings for Pine Script v6.

Fully compatible with any chart zoom and U.S. equity intraday timeframes.

author: Brute7
content:
<@739730143693766676> Great question! The S/R levels in this script are classic Floor Pivots, which are always calculated from just the previous regular session’s high, low, and close—not by looking for swing points or historical pivots “to the left.” This makes them uniformly spaced and mathematically derived, not price action-based.

This style is perfect for scalping and intraday trading, because it gives you instantly actionable levels that reflect the market structure from the most recent session—great for gaps, mean reversion, and fast trades at key S/R zones right from the open.

There’s a ton of room to customize

You could script additional pivots using longer historical data (multi-day, multi-week, or even price-action “look left” swing pivots).

You can also add as many custom levels or dynamic pivot algorithms as you want, depending on your strategy.

If you want a “look left” style (true swing or historical pivots), that’s a very different type of script—totally possible, but would need to be designed to scan X bars back for true support/resistance.

This one is focused on consistency, speed, and using “yesterday’s map” for today’s trades.

**However, I like your suggestion and I will release v4.0 version to plot the pivots and S/R levels from the last 10 sessions as well.**

author: Brute7
content:
<@1148249290293116990>:
The gap line and label in the script are specifically designed to show the difference between the previous day’s close and the current day’s 9:30am open.
It should not be referencing the previous day’s open; the true “gap” is defined as “current session’s open at 9:30am ET minus previous session’s close.”
If the gap label appears at the wrong price or isn’t updating correctly, it’s usually due to chart timing (e.g., before the new session starts) or a possible visualization glitch, especially on non-intraday timeframes.

After the new session opens at 9:30am ET, the gap label should appear at the current session’s open, showing the value “current open – previous close.”

Just published new version (v4.0) with ON/OFF toggles for all key levels!

What’s New in v4.0:

ON/OFF Switches for Every Level:
Easily toggle the display of each key level (Prev High, Prev Low, Prev Close, Pivot, R1, R2, R3, S1, S2, S3, and Gap) directly from the indicator’s settings panel.

Improved Usability:
Show only the lines and labels you care about—customize your workspace and reduce chart clutter instantly.

No Visual Duplicates:
All objects are managed cleanly: turning a level OFF fully hides its line/label, with no overlap or ghosting.

<@739730143693766676>:
I’m working on adding 10-day key levels to the same indicator script so you can plot historical key levels from the last 10 sessions.
Since this will significantly expand the code and potentially affect chart performance, I’ll publish it as a separate indicator.

author: GoodVibez
content: I consider it every single time. Tony and Jack often mention sizing for zero. It's why strength/momentum is very important. I will often buy an extra week out just to help account for some of the loss due to theta if I see something on HTF, but it doesn't move right away and chops around, but never hits my stop. Yes, buying further out requires a little more of your capitial and puts more of it at risk should the trade move against you w/ lots of strength. Why we have stops though right. But the plus side, is that if you have conviction in your HTF thesis and just feel it needs a little more time to play out, it gives you the ability to hold a trade longer and not have Theta eat as much liquiditiy as fast. This may be beneficial for someone who doesn't have a margin account, has limited funds, and is limited the amount of day trades they can enter w/n a week. They don't have the luxury of opening and closing as many trades.

author: Brute7
content:
It’s officially been one month since I started my intraday trading journey — and it all began with inspiration from the Scarface YouTube channel. What started as simple curiosity quickly turned into a daily commitment to grow, learn, and show up — regardless of profits or losses.

Like many beginners, I dove into breakout, retest, and price action strategies, thinking I had a solid handle on options trading. But over time, I realized there’s so much more to it — market news, economic reports, index correlations, relative strength, option Greeks, and key levels all play a critical role. One of my early mistakes was spreading my attention too thin, tracking too many tickers without focusing deeply on any.

So three days ago, I made a shift: I committed to just five tickers — SPX, NVDA, TSLA, QQQ, and SPY. Since then, I’ve seen a clear difference. Studying these names daily has helped me understand their behavior better, enter trades with more precision, and exit with more confidence.

Today, I hit my personal all-time high in trading. It’s not $10k (yet), but I know I’m on the right path — one day at a time.

author: John H
content: I don't think someone else's contract size matters. We have to size to our own accounts and the risk we're willing to take with each trade. For example, your risk might be $100 where Jack & Tony may be at $1000.  So, think in terms of if they bought 50 option contracts, they could only lose $.20 before they hit their max loss. If you wanted $.20 of risk equaling $100 then you could only buy 5 contracts. And, $.20 can happen in a split second. That's why knowing where you are getting out, and sizing appropriately to the number of contracts you can buy is important. Your stop might be at $.50, which would then mean only 2 contracts. Hope this makes sense. At the end of the day, it's not apples to apples to try and compare our trade size to what they are doing.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Viper - this is definitely a loaded question lol.
Hard to answer without seeing more of your data or trading patterns, but here are a few thoughts that might help:

**Plan your levels + scenarios**
If you don’t have a mapped path for where 3R–5R could come from (HTF levels, gaps, reclaim zones), it’s hard to stay in the trade. Build the scenario before you enter.

**Revisit the HOD scale**
If you're trading a small number of cons and scaling at 1R/HOD, you're likely choking the trade by taking a large % off before the trade really gets moving. That said, don’t throw out the HOD rule blindly -  let structure & context guide 

**Mind your strike**
Pay attention to where underlying price is relative to your strike when you hit HOD. A lot of the juice in options comes from delta expansion past the strike - not just tagging it.

**Trailer logic **
Even something simple like bar-by-bar, 9EMA, or last HL break helps you stay in runners. Without a plan, it's easier to flatten early.

**Journaling & Expectations**
Which setups actually run 3-5R+? Even if you scaled out early, make sure you're tracking which trades run. Not all of them do. Also keep in mind that even if the max R is 5R, most likely you won't be able to capture the full 5R due to scaling into pops. I try to aim for 50-60% "R capture" as a metric I track regularly. 

Let me know if you want to walk through a specific trade. feel free throw one up in <#1219021113686888478>  and tag me - happy to dive in.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)

Wanted to share a quick thought 🎯

The very first setup I made mine since joining this community was the Break and Retest of Previous Day High and Low. It was the one that just made sense early on, and I’ve stuck with it ever since.

If you’re watching price action today, you’ll notice how many tickers pulled back right into PDH and are now making their continuation moves. Clean, simple, and repeatable.

I’ve learned to be totally okay with just waiting for those continuation setups—no need to force anything or chase reversals. The market gives you the play if you’re patient enough to wait for it. 👊

If you're also focused on this setup, let’s share charts and build even more conviction around it! @everyone

author: Kam
content:
Hey Sean - this is a great question, and one I’ve spent a lot of time thinking about recently.

Honestly, it sounds like you’re doing a lot of the right things already. You’ve built structure around risk, setup clarity, journaling, and daily reflection - really solid foundation.

When I think about consistency, I try to break it down into a few core lenses (credit <@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> <@1014389514141184074> and Mark Douglas!)

Are your best setups clearly defined and **repeatable**?

Are you avoiding trades outside your system - even when they “look good”?

Are you managing risk the same way every time - not just when you're confident, but when you're unsure too?

You mentioned you’ve been trading this way for ~6 months. One thing I’ve been using personally is a ‘trading block’ model - aiming for 20 trades aligned with my process out of every 25. It helps me stay honest about process over PnL. If I hit it, I earn the right to pay myself with a small size bump. If not, I reset. It’s been a helpful way to compound responsibly as I've had issues sizing up too quickly in the past. 

25 trades for me typically maps to about a month’s worth of trading, but the calendar isn’t the point - the reps are.

So yeah IMO, it's not about waiting X months or years. It’s about proving to yourself that your process is repeatable. Hope that helps!

i listen to a lot of mindset videos in youtube. Today I heard something caused a shift in my thinking. It is to ask this question before entering any trade:

author: Hayden
content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

author: Jose96
content: Hi bro, this is what i do: my stop level is always somewhere where trade is invalidated and my risk is a dollar amount (in my case 100$ is my risk this month), so what i do is i theorically calculate the number of contracts so that when my stoploss hits i lose around 100$, but the level where i would lose 100$ is offesetted to the level that invalidates my trade, that way i can hold the trade until the candle closes and not exceed the 100$ risk amount(most of the time). For example, i enter a call and the level that invalidates my trade is 180, i will size my trade in a way that i will lose 100$ at 179.8 for example (it will depend on the range of the candles of that particular day), but will stop out on a candle close below 180, the extra 20cents is just to not get wicked out at the key level. I’m not experienced but maybe this makes a some sense to you

content: After reading and listening quite a bit on trading mentality, I think I wll keep using a hard stop loss based on a point of invalidation of the trade that I can both afford and that it makes sense to the PA. Mental stop loss, causes pain avoidance and promotes a “hope” which leads to a bloodbath.. We have to think not in on trade but in 50-100 trades. A hard stop loss will keep my losses small while I work on letting my winners ride. Overtime this will lead to a profit factor above 1. This is what matters, not the win rate. If this does not drive my profit factor up (keeping losses small), then I have a problem with my entries and my trade plan that I would need to review but only after my metrics of dozens of trades tell me so.

author: FierceTiger
content:
I am just going to share my trade thesis on TSLA: TSLA is a high-conviction intraday short if it becomes red (i.e. crosses previous day's close) tomorrow or anytime in the week, after this parabolic move up. It is the classic "First Red Day" setup. I think that the best way to play this setup is to short it with everything you got once price crosses from green to red, be out 80% of your position on the first green candle (first consolidation), and leave 20% runners (sell 10% EOD and leave 10% expecting Day 2 continuation). 
Point of invalidation (stop loss) is previous day's close, if it reclaims. But highly unlikely it reclaims on a "First Red Day" setup.

author: FierceTiger
content: It depends on the definition. Some would define "First Red Day" as a break below the initial opening gap price, some would define it as a break below previous day's close. I did short and banked when TSLA broke below the opening gap price today. However, true First Red Day moves are usually larger in % loss and have continuation. I think there will be a more violent move downwards coming.

author: Markellwhite16
content: you got this! you should read "trading in the zone" by Mark Douglas he emphasis on a lot pf things , one being there are no correlations between trades. yesterday has nothing to do with today and today has nothing to do with tomorrow. very important to not let past trades disrupt presents one. And mainly he says "the ultimate goal as a trader is to not only trade with a carefree state of mind but to trade without reservation and hesitation". if you see your setup and take it i promise you the market will pay you more than you pay it! and if you lose that trade it was A "good loss".

author: Alessandro
content:
Hi everyone!
Sorry, I hope I’m not being too long-winded…
I’m getting into options, and even though I went through the dedicated module, there’s still something I don’t fully understand.
In every video, Jack and Tony show a chart where a break & retest happens, followed by an R:R. For example, the one that just happened on Nvidia could have been a trade with an R:R of 1:9 — meaning with $100 risk I could’ve made $900. I don’t think I’ve misunderstood up to this point, but please correct me if I have…
That said, they apply the trade on options. I was looking at the puts, and the first OTM was the $170 strike (with the live price being just above $171 at that moment). I don’t remember the exact BID and ASK, but they were around $2.00 per contract, let’s round it for simplicity (so the premium to pay to enter the buy put was $200).
The trade on TradingView would’ve closed at $169.50, and at that point the BID/ASK was around $2.85 per contract. So, assuming I bought 1 contract, I’d have risked $200 to close the trade at $285. That’s an $85 profit (after commissions, maybe around $80 net).
Am I missing something here?
Where does the R:R from the videos come into play? Because this contract would’ve ended up with an R:R of just 1.4 
Thank you

author: Jdub
content:
This is not an alert service. 

If you want alerts this is not the place to be. 

What matters is the risk amount and thesis. 

If you just copy alerts it doesn't work because you don't understand the thesis and logic behind the trade. 

Even if you get the same entries ultimately if you don't build your own thesis and confidence you won't be able to hold onto the trade if it works out because your reliant on someone else besides your self.

author: Funkdamentalist
content:
This is what I've noticed Tony and Jack do, and makes a lot of sense. Scale out 25% or 50% at first target, then another 25% and let the rest run until it breaks the trend.

I can usually stick to my 1:2 RR confidently... and then I exit. THEN I'll watch the price end up at like 1:4 or more RR and it can be frustrating to watch. 

My problem right now is, I only have enough in the account for one to two contracts per day... so I can't really do the 50, 25, 25 option.  Also it would mean I would need to get in at 100% on the get go... and I'm still building the confidence enough to go to 100% max shares off the bat.

I know the theory is that the trade I should be taking is an A+ setup which should give me the confidence to go max shares with multiple contracts (at least 4) so I can do the 50%, 25%, 25% scaling out.... I'm just not there yet

author: G Nice
content: If I only have 2 contracts, then I would not scale at HoD. Scale 1 at a key level (ideally 2:1 min target) then let the other run until break of 5m structure. When trading 1-3 contracts, focus on the R:R and improving on entry/setups instead of  PnL

author: Markellwhite16
content: I see where you’re coming from. A couple  of things .. never predict . Our job is to react not predict. When you predict you go into the market with a bias and force trades. I am 100% bullish on PLTR until it breaks a key level on the HTF that causes a break of structure. You don’t wanna be the trader that keeps trying to short the highs and get smoked. The trend is your friend is a real thing. Look at names like MU , ORCl , even QQQ & SPY. Week in and out these names keep making new highs. PLTR can easily rip through its ATH , pull back , retest that level and continue higher. The last 2 days on this name has been a ROCKET 🚀! So my best advice is don’t go into the market trying to “predict” the exact top where a reversal might occur. Just keep taking what the market gives you.

author: Markellwhite16
content: I promise you will always lose more than you will win trying short the highs. I guarantee you there are some traders who keep saying “SPY is so extended” and they keep trying to short it On a pull back type of day and just gets smoked 💨. If you really wanna short  you have to wait for a break of structure on the HTF. Hope this helps bud 🤞🏾🤙🏾

author: Markellwhite16
content: Of course! Glad I could help. Don’t beat yourself up. LEARN FROM YOUR MISTAKES. I failed my way to success. “The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again expecting a different outcome” . Keep it simple . Understand HTF context , when you see a bullish & trending stock pulling  back don’t see it as “short it” see it as “where can I go long on the dip in order to catch this trend to the upside. Also we’re in a bullish market! Don’t fight it , join it! Don’t be a bear bias right  now because we’re not in a bearish market at all. You got this 👌🏽

author: Angel (az)

TSLA when it doesn't work out typically is a 20% loss of total cost, so if you went full port, that sounds about right. You also want to keep in mind how many cons you are trading on other names. Even if you win usually but usually your total capital is not the full $2000, it won't cover your 20% loss on the $2000 even at 2R bc you might get unlucky on the big trade

author: Markellwhite16
content: Just a Reminder ❤️ trading is all probability not certainties . EVREY trade we take we don’t know if it’s gonna be a loser or a winner. Wins and losses doesn’t make you a good trader. What makes a great trader is someone who follow their rules , system , and stay discipline. You can’t judge your performance off PnL but based off those things ☝🏾. ALWAYS keep a long term mindset. Can’t focus on one day. Focus on results at the end of the week , month , quarter , and year. Always keep the long term mindset! If you lost today . author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Luke
content:
Since they're 0DTE contracts I play them a lot differently than I would futures contracts.
a) I'm still developing and refining my playbook, but what I usually do is trail it by the break of structure on the 1min after a decent move where the contracts are at least 15-20%. When there is a flush and the contracts start to move, market structure starts to really play out and be respected.
b) On these setups a lot of times when it consolidates during the trade and starts to stall and not move down, there can be really impulsive green candles which crush the contracts. They're usually easy to spot within the first few seconds of the candle because buyers aggressive start stepping in. In that case I'll usually stop out once candle just gets above my invalidation level. What I'm trying to work on right now is not letting green trades go red.
Hopefully that makes sense let me know if it does.

author: Jerry
content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

author: Luke
content:
With these trades I scale them purely based on the underlying key levels and not the contract's percentage. How I personally use the contract's percentage to my advantage so avoid unnecessary losses is once i'm in at least 15-20% ish profit (which if you get a move down right away will automatically happen) I don't want to make it a loss so I won't hold to my stop loss. The only time I will hold to my stop loss is if the trade doesn't work out right away. So like at my entry here you can see I wanted to scale 40% at 659, but it didn't hit it and rejected, and my contracts went to around breakeven. If there was any strength after that 10:17 candle I would've stopped out immediately with a super small loss (pretty much breakeven).
As far as the R:R tool goes on the underlying chart. With 0DTEs you can't really base it off that much because they move way differently. Sometimes on the underlying you'll be up 1:1R but the contracts are up almost 2R. One thing I personally will do is if the contracts move to 40% and i'm not at TP1 yet, i'll scale some no matter what because that's like a 2R on the contracts because on these type of trades you should be losing about 15-20% on your losses.
So for your first trade that was up 30%, if I hadn't hit TP 1 yet then I would for sure have stopped out when the contracts hit breakeven, because you can always look to re-enter, so just save yourself from a loss and reevaluate. For your second trade, if it hadn't hit TP 1 yet and the contracts were at 30%, then I would've stopped out breakeven. How I play it is when the contracts are at 40% (2R for the contracts) and price rejects, i'll sell the contracts at 15-20% in profit no matter what so that it doesn't become a red trade.
Those are just some rules i've made for myself over the past weeks of trading thse, hopefully that helps

The thing is that options do not behave like the underlying asset, but only very similarly. However, we do not trade the underlying asset and use the option merely as leverage but we use the underlying as our „road map“. So just put two buttons together. Buy Market 
Sell market 

Don’t Look on the option chart. Buy when the underlying chart tells you to buy and exit when the underlying chart tells you again (by stop loss or take profit)

author: healthiswealth
content: I had this same question as well. I was winning, but the losses were twice as big. What I noticed was that I was not scaling out my wins properly. Also, I didn't have trailers. Having trailers are HUGE in order to maximize a win. There have been trades where Tony and JDub have made more from the trailers than they did at the first and second scale outs. So, when we don't scale out properly and have trailers, losses will be greater than the wins because we don't match Tony/JDub's exact winning formula and we "leave meat on the bone", but we do match exactly the formula when they take a loss.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Here is a really sinple method I use as an options trader for scalping,

a)- What is the max loss per trade I am willing to accept today given my account size?

b)- when the set up is forming, I watch where my TPs are, I take a TP1 and also the ultimate, TP1 to me should be 1:2, the ultimate is whatever it can be. Understand this is a super conservative  (but game changer) approach. 

c) - Here is the science, what is the underlying (real stock) $cents size of the most realistic stop loss (not an arbitrary one)??
d)
Formula: 

 c) x contracts number x 0.5 = a). 

Example : Stop loss measures  25 cts of stock value. 
Max loss per trade is $50
Factor is 0.5
So,
25 x 0.5 = 12.5
$50 / 12.5 = 4 contracts. 

That is your contract size to lose $50 as per your measured stop loss (as long as you exit when it says so). 

then, 

Your RvR  ratio to TP1 is 1:2
So,

You have to measure  and find 50 stock value cents to TP1 to enter the trade.

Notice I measure 1:2 RvR to TP1, yes I know this is conservative, but not really cause you are only interested anyways in a  small stop loss, so provided this is what you look for, a RvR to TP1 of 1:2 is realistic and it will occur often. 

Hope this helps, with this method you will be more focused on the  size of your stop loss rather than % of your account to discard set ups that are not winners in what you need: Lower risk

author: Royal191
content: Hi there, we are a learning community first. There are a lot of educational content in here including the accelerator course which is not available on youtube or anywhere else. I would highly recommend you spend some time going through the videos. Feel free to skip the beginner stuff if you like. There are also trade reviews and weekly sessions at your disposal. The live trading is for you to see in real time how the strategy is executed and learn in real time as well. You can follow along and potentially take the same trades as Jack and Tony as they are pretty clear when and why they are entering a trade. They call out which name they are looking at and the levels. But you do need to learn the entry models so you can execute yourself and have a good entry with a better risk to reward. IF you are waiting to hear them say they enter then you will always be a few seconds late which can make a big difference since we are trading momentum and some of these moves happen quickly.

author: wakefieldcapital
content:
**WHY DIDN'T NVDA WORKOUT TODAY?**

Here's my small thesis, might be wrong but moderators can provide their thoughts as well. While going over the average NVDA move from 9AM to 11AM for the last 1 year, it seems to always be around 1-2%. 
Considering the steep move today (with no green candles) from market open, I feel we have already made a big displacement and the momentum for the first half of the day was almost completed (around 2.5%). Moreover, right below the 5min ORL we did also have the 180 HTF and psych level that did represent a big liquidity zone with buyers aggressively pushing up which led to the retest. 
So, considering these 2 key points, I feel NVDA didn't workout in our favours. Guess most of us did take that trade today so thought I'd share my small feedback on it.

author: Kam
content:
Unfortunately the answer is it 'depends' - usually on the type of setup, HTFs , market context etc. On Reclaim trades I take less off (for me less is 10% scale) or none at HOD cos those tend to move aggressively in my favor and I don't want to choke off the trade prematurely. On retests, it depends how much the stock has moved already. If the retest is earlier in the session I'll take less off at HOD etc. 

Keep in mind if trading generally smaller size, each con counts - the math tends to work better if  let our trades run - but def need to backtest and look at your live data to find the answer that works best for you!

author: Jonathan
content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.

author: JABARI
content:
Giving yourself 6 months to come to a conclusion like this isn’t realistic. Trading takes time, just like starting any other business or going to university to get a degree. It’s not a get rich quick scheme. I’m not sure what expectations you had coming into trading, but even trading 1-3 times a week already puts you at a bit of a disadvantage when you’re day trading. You need to be attending every trading day to build that momentum.

As for the trades they take, the announce it as soon as they take it, but options move very quickly and put your entry at a disadvantage especially when you’re waiting for someone else to give you a signal through a livestream. On top of that your broker matters as well with how they’re filling your order. in regards to the candles, there are different entry criteria’s they’ve went through extensively and it’s the same one they follow, the difference is they’re way more experienced with candle formations, discretion, and their edge that they will enter sooner than you can think to enter. 
You can always compare your entry time to the livestream they upload and see if it lines up, and I can assure you it will line up. 

Another thing about tony making 25k+ is because of his position size and the way he scales those cons - you’re not able to do that with your account size and psychology at this time unfortunately. It’s better you continue practising with 3 cons and always leave the last one as a runner so you can build experience or you can always paper trade using more cons to practise scaling.

Stay consistent, give yourself more room and time and be optimistic.

author: Dee
content: That's very tough to hear.  Trading is an individual journey and 6 months is not enough time to see consistency and if we are being really honest with ourselves, the live trading is a process that works for them  because it their business. Even if we were all in the same room, no one is  going to get into the trade at the same exact time. The best thing you can do is to try and gain conviction for yourself, so you can have some accountability. It sucks when you follow their trade and you lose, vs when you follow your own idea and you lose money. Give yourself the opportunity to learn from your mistakes and figure out your own style

author: Masanda
content: I like 1dte because it allows me to absorb a little more consolidation should the trade thesis still be intact and it just take a little longer to work.  I also prefer being on the 5 min chart since my own personality can get whipsawed on the 1 min chart.  BNR from key level to key level and scale, scale, scale.  Also, not making a move until almost 30 mins after opens really helps me see more clearly after the opening dust has settled.  It's so easy to overpay for premiums that'll come in a little bit if you can wait an extra 15 mins for the setup to happen.

author: 305 Trader
content:
good morning Patel. Do not go hard on yourself. Your value is not defined by 2x losses. Not by 10x or even 20x losses. If you develop a trading plan and you adhere to it, that discipline will define you. Trading is a business where focus on zoom out and zoom in is needed. Fight FOMO, a falling move can also be a falling knife. This particular dump in SPY in its first leg as per your screenshot did not offer an A+ set up in my view for the following reasons:

- 1st reaction candle, too large. Increasing the Stop loss unnecessarily if you like to wait for a confirmation candle.
- your 2nd set up, no proper mitigation, just friction and the entry at LOD. 

“Not everything that shines is gold”, its an old spanish saying. Become picky, selective and forget about the profits that people post everyday, it is your journey and your money.

author: 305 Trader
content: I wanted to give you a heads up on 0DTE. I spent probsbly an hour with Charles Schwab on getting some explanation from the Greeks on my loss. The underlying QQQ moved 50 cts adversely but my loss was 40 cts in that same range.  This means an 80% loss in value to $1 of the underlying value in just 5 mins trade. Charles Schwab says that market makers do not like to explain the reason why DELTA is "undefined" and "non-relevant" their words on 0DTE. They encouraged me to use 1 DTE if I could, becuase DELTA then is "more relevant".  thats how far their help went. So, the lesson is, if trading 0DTE, be very picky with the Stop Loss while it is kept real. Because in my case it was just 50 cts Stop loss but the outcome was unexpectedly at .80 DELTA. Also, the IV goes higher as you further away from the ATM, keep that in mind too. I was on the 3rd slot at OTM.

author: 305 Trader
content: And, this loss was at 09:55 EST so  Theta did not really play a role and the trade lasted only 5 mins. Volume was mid range to low.

author: Masanda
content: Yeah it's pretty much why I stopped with 0DTEs and stick with 1DTE.  I can accept the tradeoff of stability over only slightly less gains compared to 0DTE.

author: 305 Trader
content: Yes,  I agree. this is probably the extreme case what I experienced, but since it occurred it means it can happen when someone is in with 50 cons instead of 3 cons as I was. So, best advice, only A+ set ups meaning with a RvR that has particularly a low stop loss which I would not make it higher than 30 cts ETF and real, not convenient.

author: EeveeWinter
content: They rarely trade SPY and QQQ - stocks don’t have 0DTE unless it’s Friday. They trade the weekly expiration 1 OTM, sometimes 2 OTM depending on volume. On Friday they will play the zeroes but sometimes at a lighter size

author: EeveeWinter
content: No worries - it’s definitely something to keep an eye on when trading stocks vs indices - on Monday and Tuesday, the option premiums are much more expensive but are more forgiving in time because of theta but Thursday and Friday the contracts are much cheaper but also deceptive because the theta is on steroids (especially Friday) and your trade basically has to work right away or your loss is going to be a lot bigger than you expected

author: Dan Grand
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

author: Bhargav
content:
After experiencing a massive drawdown in August, September, & October, I lost around $8000. So I decided to sit back and take a break. While I took the break, I went all in and backtested the system and developed my psychology, and saw some great results. First, I backtested the strategy on QQQ ( 3 Months Data ), and then I tested it on NVDA ( 3 Months Data ). Then, looking at the data, I understood the way I traded and what works for me. 


So I started again on the 12th of November with $500 and already grew my small account to $5448. Only traded 5 days and took 1 trade a day, that’s it, and you can see the results.

author: Hayden

Sure 👍 

Truth is, numbers alone don’t show the full story. They don’t show the bad trades I passed on, the discipline it took to sit on my hands, or the weeks where protecting capital mattered more than making it. Mostly important is the reasons why 

P&L can create the wrong focus, invite comparison, and push people into forcing trades just to “keep up"

But I also believe in transparency. Everyday I do coaching and talk about my setups / PNL / scaling and why I didn't take trades. If you really want something that's going to help, those sessions and thoughts will go a lot further than numbers will 😅

author: Joebag009
content: Thank you for taking the time to reply to my specific question, hearing that from a pro means a lot! I have noticed psychologically I start getting nervous when positions are over 8K, even if a good chunk of my total account is not deployed. My capital is 30K and that gives me 100K buy power in the margin account. I try not to have any position over 10K, except for plain shares of indexes in a day trade (slower move = same risk as small leveraged position). Positions of 2-6K are a comfort zone, and at that size risking 50 to make 100 hits hard! On a day trade if I see it -50 I get nervous and if it is close to +100 I'm pumping that "sell" button like a senior at a casino machine handle. I am starting to get comfortable with a single option on QQQ. Compared to the pro traders who do one or two setups a day, I take many many trades with ridiculously low risk tolerance and quick cashouts. It is tedious but has given me consistent green week after week. I hope to learn to trade more like the pros, upping my skills to pick better setups and have the confidence to let them ride to bigger reward ratios. After just a day in this group, I can already tell it will accelerate my growth. Happy thanksgiving to everyone, I'm so grateful today to be part of a trading community like this, not just out in the "wild" of Youtube where discussion is super limited

author: Joebag009
content: By your account - if you like the idea. I'm only a few months into trading, but let me tell you a quick story. I followed the warrior trading guy for a while, and a few other influencers. In the early weeks of trading, everything that I understood seemed like the way to go if the presenter was making cash. Now I know that if it is true for them, it might not be true for me. The 84% thing might be true for Scarface, but it could just lead me or you to chasing a setup we see in retrospect. It happens every week that I exit at a tiny loss/breakeven/tiny gain only for the a*** to explode up 5 minutes later on the next leg.... but you know what, every once in a while, it also dumps the other way sooo hard it would have been a soul crushing loss. I will rather live to fight another day or take a small win, miss those upsides, and AVOID those soul crushing losses... so, does the 84% thing speak to your trading experience, and what are your trading rules? Exposure to the rules of other traders, I think, helps us develop our own trading styles and rules : )

author: EeveeWinter
content: Small account, which I did at the start it’s very hard to scale the way Tony and Jack do. If you’ve got 2 contracts, I used to scale 1 at HOD and have the 2nd one aim for 2RR. HOD doesn’t always offer 2RR. But if it does, even better trade to take. It makes it easier to hold 50% of your position once you’ve gotten 50% of it secured. And you should journal and backtest your win rate.

author: EeveeWinter
content: Sometimes with a small account, certain trades just need to be all out at HOD, you’ll need to keep a higher win rate, but preserving capital is key. Keep a tight rein on stop loss. That was my biggest downfall early. If you can get 2RR consistently, you’re winning. The 3R and up don’t show up everyday, and yes you can let one runner go if you’ve got it available -

author: Das Wookie
content:
<@1014389514141184074> you asked if everyone understood why the importance of the $686 key level on SPY was so important, and the rejection off of it. That's not what was rejected tho. This might just be you rounding, but the key level for me was 685.73... not $686. Maybe this is a "Yeah but it's close enough" kinda thing... so you don't mean EXACTLY $686, but basically... my key level wasn't there.

The $686 key level was from HOD in the pre-market, but we didn't quite get there before the price rejected off of $685.73 and headed down.

$685.73 was a key level from HOD on Market Open back on the 4th, and we had a hard bounce off of it yesterday in the initial spike in price from the FOMC release. There was a tight consolidation between $685.73 and $686 in pre-market as well as after hours back on the 5th.

A $685.73 key level seems to have been more important based on the price rejection yesterday, but I just figured that was a level where that initial momentum gave out. $686 is an interesting key level, but mostly from Extended Hours trading.

So, not to be pedantic, but the $686 key level, specifically... in regards to THAT trade... well... uhmmmm.... no. Not really.

The $682.40 level would have been the more interesting one to me for that trade, as that was LOD for pre-market, and as I was watching prices fall, that's the key level I would have been watching VERY closely for a rejection and flip. I was trading the AAPL drop at the time, so the price I was watching for was over there. On the way up tho for the rebound, I quickly shot in some key levels for me to watch close (bottom of steps going down) as potential rejection and reversion points... and traded that on it's way up.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
I want to add some perspective here.
I’ve personally witnessed Neto grow into a highly consistent trader over the past year+. He didn’t just “show up” one day — he put in the work, learned from his mistakes, and earned his role as a coach/moderator through contribution and consistency. He has openly acknowledged his trade wins and his trade struggles over time, from small trades to larger ones.
Regarding P&L:
Sharing exact profit numbers is personal financial information. No trader — moderator or not — is obligated to disclose that. Some days we share contract size or P&L, some days we don’t. I do the same. In fact, I intentionally stopped sharing profit screenshots once trades exceed ~$2–3k because it can negatively impact newer traders’ psychology and motivation.
Cutting off P&L does not equal deception. It often equals:
protecting personal financial privacy
preventing unhealthy comparison
keeping focus on execution, process, and structure — not dollar amounts
This community is about education, discipline, and consistency, not flexing numbers or fueling speculation.

author: Dan Grand
content:
What a great day it was here in the chat 😂
Let’s switch the subject 😉

I want to share how this month is going from my side.
I am BE!

From green to zero — and I actually feel great. Why? Because I feel the progress I’ve made since the moment I found Tony on YouTube and joined this community. I’m still developing my edge, but I stick to my rules. My progress has really accelerated since I got here.

Before I met Tony, I was coming to the charts without really understanding what I was supposed to do. What was my strategy at all? 😂
I was hoping the 20 SMA would tell me which button to press 😂
Now I’m kind of laughing when I think about the early days of my journey.

I went through two mentorships and never even saw their performance… not sure why I chose them. Probably because they were posting charts and adding +$$$$ amounts to their stories every single day. As a new player in the industry, that consistency made me believe them.

Now I understand what I’m actually looking for. I have a clear strategy, and I feel like I’m in the most important stage of the process — the BE stage.
This is where mistakes happen, sometimes good profits happen, and sometimes really good losses happen (meaning even if the trade is bad from an HTF context — which is my weakest point because I never worked on it before — I still respect my SL). And i am barely or even not at all lose money 💰

content:

Premarket prep, trade reviews, coaching sessions — every single day people here are doing their best to provide real knowledge.

Over the past six months, even before I officially joined the community, just from Scarface’s YouTube content (and later discovering JDub on YouTube and realizing they’re partners), I’ve made huge progress.

Long term, I truly see myself winning if I keep showing up consistently over the next year and sticking to the rules.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
===========================
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

@everyone   

Please confirm you have read this with a 🤑

author: Luke
content: Hey guys, was backtesting NVDA and thought I'd post this perfect example of the "1st Pullback Rule." You can see that NVDA had lots of strength above its Previous Day High and had all green candles for the first 5 mins with Qs pushing up as well. Usually the first pullback has the highest probability of getting bought up into HOD and usually will pullback into the 9EMA as well. This is from Oct 21, 2024 if anyone wants to go to it. author: Hugo Comte
content:
Hi everyone.

Transitionning from stocks to options is tricky atm  as i have issues with contracts/sizing and id really like some help to understand how to size properly and pick the right contracts, as im not able to TP correctly atm.

Todays trade was a long on AMZN.
I got in at 242.8 with 4 contracts of the 242.5

with the risk reward simulator of Trading view, 400 shares ( 4 contracts ) is  a 300$ risk on my stop stop loss ,
However at 1R i was only in profit of 190$ ( just 0.65R ) 

Why is that ?
Should i have taken the 245 contracts ? the move should have been 3R in order to be in the money.
or buy more contract ? but why doesn't it correlates  with the defined risk in $

author: Hugo Comte
content:
Got it ! my issue was the delta not being accounted, meaning i was basically half the size.

Whats your actual process when you want to place a trade with the right size with a defined risk in $.

Lets say you want to risk 100$?  i would do the following:

- Place a stop where i want to exit on the underlying.
- define the % risk on the underlying, lets say 1$
- Mutiply by my total risk > 100$ > 100 Shares.
- 100 shares > 1 contracts 
- corect size based on Delta 0.5 > 2 contracts

Thatts just so much step in order to define sizing when i you want to be very quick with your entry

author: Luke
content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

author: Aniketh Singh
content:
Overview
Nasdaq has received approval to list and trade options with Monday and Wednesday expirations for a select group of high-liquidity stocks and ETFs. This program will officially begin on January 26, 2026.
Qualified Securities (Q1 2026)
The following nine assets have been selected for this program during the first quarter of 2026:
1. AAPL (Apple Inc.)
2. AMZN (Amazon.com Inc.)
3. AVGO (Broadcom Inc.)
4. GOOGL (Alphabet Inc.)
5. IBIT (iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF)
6. META (Meta Platforms Inc.)
7. MSFT (Microsoft Corp.)
8. NVDA (NVIDIA Corp.)
9. TSLA (Tesla Inc.)
Key Details & Restrictions
• Earnings Exclusions: To prevent excessive volatility risks, Monday or Wednesday expirations will not be listed if they coincide with a company's scheduled earnings announcement. For instance:
• AAPL: Excluded around Jan 29, 2026.
• MSFT, META, TSLA: Excluded for Jan 28, 2026.
• Selection Criteria: Stocks must have a market capitalization exceeding $700 billion, and ETFs must meet specific Assets Under Management (AUM) and trading volume thresholds.
• Quarterly Updates: Nasdaq will review and update this list every quarter to ensure all participating securities still meet the required financial standards.
This change is significant for options traders as it provides more precise tools for hedging and speculation on the world's largest tech companies and Bitcoin.

author: Jatin
